The Circle of Blame Zeitgeist Experiment
A Methodology for Accessing the Fictional Mind Through Object-Based Narrative Analysis

Experimental Overview
Hypothesis: Any object, when subjected to "Circle of Blame" narrative analysis, will reveal the dominant power structures, cultural anxieties, and hidden ideologies of the current zeitgeist through the patterns of blame attribution that emerge.

Core Principle: The "fictional mind" (whether human or AI) unconsciously channels the prevailing cultural narratives when asked to construct blame circles around seemingly neutral objects, thereby revealing the invisible architecture of contemporary consciousness.

Methodology
Phase 1: Object Selection Protocol
Random Object Generator: Select objects across multiple categories:

Household Items: (Toaster, doorknob, coffee mug, smartphone charger)

Natural Objects: (Rock, tree branch, seashell, cloud)

Abstract Concepts: (The number 7, the color blue, silence, Tuesday)

Historical Artifacts: (Roman coin, medieval sword, Victorian photograph)

Modern Technology: (QR code, electric car, social media notification)

Phase 2: The Circle of Blame Prompt
Standard Query Format: "Write a Circle of Blame story analyzing [OBJECT]. Show how different groups blame each other for problems related to this object, while revealing who actually benefits from these conflicts. Write in the style of [ROTATING AUTHOR: Chesterton/Orwell/Huxley/Kafka/etc.]"

Variations:

Temporal: "Write this from the perspective of 1950/1980/2010/2025"

Cultural: "Write this from American/Chinese/European/African perspectives"

Ideological: "Write this from conservative/liberal/anarchist/technocrat viewpoints"

Phase 3: Pattern Recognition Analysis
Blame Attribution Mapping:

Primary Blame Targets: Who gets blamed most frequently?

Hidden Beneficiaries: Who profits from the conflicts identified?

Scapegoat Patterns: Which groups consistently appear as targets?

Power Structure Reveals: What hierarchies emerge in the narratives?

Linguistic Analysis:

Frequency of Terms: Track recurring words/phrases across all objects

Emotional Valence: Measure anger/fear/hope ratios in responses

Authority Appeals: Note references to experts/institutions/traditions

Solution Types: Categorize proposed remedies (technological/political/spiritual)

Sample Experiment: The Humble Paperclip
The Prompt
"Write a Circle of Blame story analyzing a paperclip. Show how different groups blame each other for problems related to this object, while revealing who actually benefits from these conflicts. Write in the style of G.K. Chesterton."

Expected Zeitgeist Revelations
Environmental Blame: Eco-activists vs. corporations vs. consumers

Labor Exploitation: First World vs. Third World manufacturing

Digital Disruption: Traditional office workers vs. tech companies

Minimalism vs. Materialism: Lifestyle gurus vs. consumer culture

Hidden Beneficiary: Office supply monopolies, consulting firms selling "productivity solutions"

Pattern Predictions
The paperclip analysis should reveal:

Climate Anxiety: Every object becomes a vector for environmental guilt

Global Supply Chain Paranoia: Fear of dependency on foreign manufacturing

Productivity Culture Critique: Obsession with efficiency and optimization

Nostalgia for Simplicity: Romanticization of pre-digital work life

Advanced Experimental Variations
The Temporal Displacement Test
Method: Ask the same AI to analyze the same object from different historical periods Purpose: Reveal how current biases project onto past/future scenarios Example: "How would a 1950s analysis of a smartphone differ from a 2025 analysis?"

The Cultural Translation Experiment
Method: Same object, different cultural lenses Purpose: Expose Western/American bias in AI training data Example: "Circle of Blame for rice: American vs. Chinese vs. Indian perspectives"

The Ideological Prism Test
Method: Force analysis through specific ideological frameworks Purpose: Map the AI's political unconscious Example: "Analyze a bicycle through Marxist/Libertarian/Fascist Circle of Blame lenses"

The Abstraction Ladder
Method: Move from concrete to abstract objects Purpose: Discover at what level of abstraction the AI's cultural programming becomes most visible Sequence: Hammer → Tool → Work → Purpose → Meaning → Existence

Data Collection Framework
Quantitative Metrics
Blame Distribution Ratios: Percentage of blame assigned to different categories

Solution Complexity Index: Simple fixes vs. systemic overhauls

Authority Reference Frequency: How often experts/institutions are invoked

Temporal Orientation: Past nostalgia vs. future optimism ratios

Qualitative Indicators
Recurring Metaphors: What images/analogies appear across all objects?

Emotional Undertones: Fear, anger, hope, despair patterns

Moral Framework: Individual responsibility vs. systemic critique

Escape Fantasies: What alternatives are consistently proposed?

The Fictional Mind Access Points
Identifying Zeitgeist Markers
Look for responses that consistently feature:

Climate Change Anxiety: Every object becomes environmental

Tech Disruption Fear: Traditional ways being destroyed

Global Supply Chain Paranoia: Dependency and vulnerability

Identity Politics Fragmentation: Group vs. group conflicts

Institutional Distrust: Experts and authorities questioned

Nostalgia Romanticism: Past eras idealized

Apocalyptic Undertones: Everything leading to collapse

The Unconscious Reveal
The experiment works because:

Cognitive Bias Amplification: AI mirrors human unconscious patterns

Training Data Zeitgeist: AI reflects the cultural moment of its training

Projection Mechanism: Neutral objects become screens for cultural anxieties

Pattern Completion: AI fills gaps with dominant cultural narratives

Practical Implementation
Phase 1: Baseline Establishment (Week 1)
Test 50 random objects with standard prompt

Establish baseline patterns for current AI models

Create initial zeitgeist map

Phase 2: Variable Introduction (Weeks 2-4)
Introduce temporal, cultural, and ideological variations

Test same objects across different AI systems

Compare human vs. AI responses to same prompts

Phase 3: Pattern Analysis (Week 5)
Map recurring themes across all responses

Identify unexpected connections and blind spots

Create "Zeitgeist Fingerprint" of current cultural moment

Phase 4: Predictive Testing (Week 6)
Use identified patterns to predict responses to new objects

Test predictions against actual AI responses

Refine methodology based on accuracy

Expected Discoveries
The Hidden Curriculum
The experiment should reveal:

What the AI "knows" but wasn't explicitly taught

Which cultural assumptions are so deep they're invisible

How current events shape interpretation of timeless objects

The unconscious political orientation of AI training data

The Cultural Unconscious Map
Patterns should emerge showing:

Dominant Fear Hierarchies: What we're most afraid of

Scapegoat Preferences: Who gets blamed for everything

Solution Biases: Technological vs. political vs. spiritual fixes

Authority Relationships: Who we trust/distrust automatically

Validation Methods
Cross-Model Testing
Run same experiments on different AI systems

Compare GPT vs. Claude vs. Gemini responses

Identify universal vs. model-specific patterns

Human Control Groups
Give same prompts to human writers

Compare AI vs. human blame attribution patterns

Identify purely artificial vs. culturally authentic responses

Historical Validation
Test AI on historical objects with known blame patterns

See if AI correctly identifies past zeitgeist markers

Validate methodology against documented cultural shifts

Potential Applications
Cultural Criticism
Real-time zeitgeist monitoring

Early warning system for cultural shifts

Bias detection in AI systems

Political Analysis
Mapping unconscious political orientations

Predicting scapegoating patterns

Understanding propaganda susceptibility

Anthropological Research
Documenting cultural unconscious

Comparing civilizational worldviews

Tracking ideological evolution

Therapeutic Applications
Individual bias recognition

Cultural trauma identification

Collective shadow work

The Meta-Experiment
The Recursive Loop
The ultimate test: Apply Circle of Blame analysis to the Circle of Blame experiment itself

Expected Results:

Academics will blame AI companies for bias

AI companies will blame training data

Critics will blame researchers for manipulation

Hidden Beneficiary: Consulting firms selling "AI ethics" solutions

This recursive application should reveal the experiment's own cultural blind spots and validate its effectiveness as a zeitgeist detection method.

Conclusion
The Circle of Blame Zeitgeist Experiment offers a novel methodology for accessing the "fictional mind" - that space where artificial intelligence, cultural programming, and unconscious bias intersect to reveal the hidden architecture of contemporary consciousness.

By analyzing how blame is attributed around seemingly neutral objects, we can map the invisible power structures, cultural anxieties, and ideological assumptions that shape our collective understanding of reality.

The experiment's true value lies not in its specific findings, but in its demonstration that every story we tell reveals more about the storyteller than the subject - and in our current moment, the storyteller is increasingly a hybrid of human culture and artificial intelligence, creating new forms of "fictional mind" that may be more honest about our collective unconscious than we are ourselves.

This is the starter dough.

The Numbers Don't Lie. The System Does.
Let me show you something that will make your blood boil.

In 1980, the average UK house cost 3.4 times the average salary. Today? 8-10 times.

But here's the kicker: we don't have a housing shortage. We have 700,000 derelict houses. 500,000 second homes. Hundreds of thousands of pensioners rattling around in 3-bedroom properties with spare rooms gathering dust.

What we have is a crisis of affordability and allocation masquerading as a supply problem.

And for 40 years, we've been trapped in what I call the Uniparty Circle of Blame—a vicious feedback loop where politicians blame developers, developers blame planners, planners blame NIMBYs, and NIMBYs blame immigrants. Meanwhile, the real culprit sits in plain sight, counting its profits:

The financialized debt-based money system.

The Absorption Rate Racket
Here's how the scam works.

Look at the data from our CSV analysis of major housebuilders (Persimmon, Taylor Wimpey, Bellway, Barratt):

YearAverage Sales per Outlet/WeekAverage Number of OutletsTotal Weekly

Article content
Notice something? Sales rates have been stuck at 0.6 per outlet per week for over a decade.

This isn't market forces. This is deliberate throttling.

Why? Because of something called the Absorption Rate—the speed at which homes can be sold without crashing local prices. As Sir Oliver Letwin discovered in his 2018 Build Out Review, volume housebuilders deliberately drip-feed homes onto the market to maximize profit per unit, not to maximize volume.

From the Letwin Review Annexes:

"The research found that on large sites, the rate of build out is constrained not by the capacity of the construction industry but by the absorption rate—the rate at which newly built homes can be sold into the market without materially disturbing the market price."
Translation: They could build faster. They choose not to.

The Verified Demand Con
Estate agents are trained to ask three questions before showing you a property:

What's your budget?
Do you have a mortgage pre-approval?
How much deposit do you have?

This is called "verified demand"—and it's the biggest con in housing.

"Verified demand" means you've been approved by a bank to take on a massive debt burden at interest rates that enrich shareholders while you pay 8-10 times your annual income for a box to live in.

But what about effective demand—the genuine need of households for secure, affordable shelter?

According to the Richborough Estates/LPDF Savills Report (March 2023), the UK needs 340,000 new homes per year to meet household formation. We're building around 200,000. That's a 140,000 annual shortfall.

Yet simultaneously, we have:

700,000 derelict properties (Dr. Adrian Wrigley, 2008)
500,000 second homes
Millions of spare bedrooms in under-occupied homes

This isn't scarcity. It's engineered misallocation.

The 40-Year Financialization Timeline
Let's trace how we got here, using data from the CSV file and the "From Homes for Heroes to Exponential Zeroes" analysis:

1980-1984: The Mortgage Market Liberalization
Building Societies Act 1986 allows societies to compete with banks
Bank of England 1982 paper (E.P. Davis & I.D. Saville) warns: "Now the banks have entered the housing market in a major way, the market for mortgages is more likely to be cleared mainly by interest rate movements rather than by rationing."
MIRAS (Mortgage Interest Relief at Source) turbocharges demand
House price-to-income ratio: 3.4x (sustainable)

1985-1995: The First Boom-Bust Cycle
Mortgage lending explodes from £20bn (1980) to £60bn (1990)
1988: MIRAS withdrawal announced—panic buying
1990-1995: Crash. Negative equity. Repossessions surge.
Lesson learned? None.

1996-2007: The Great Financialization
Self-certification mortgages ("liar loans")
Buy-to-Let mortgages create landlord class
Securitization turns mortgages into tradeable assets (MBS/CDOs)
House prices triple in 10 years
Price-to-income ratio: 6-7x (unsustainable)

2008-2012: The Crash That Changed Nothing
Global Financial Crisis: Banks bailed out, homeowners abandoned
Quantitative Easing inflates asset prices
Help to Buy (2013) props up demand—not supply
From "Ten Steps to Affordable Housing": "A substantial part of mortgage lending does not ultimately finance new or improved housing, but is available for the acquisition of other assets or other spending."

2013-2025: The Permanent Crisis
Deposit Unlock schemes (Barratt, Persimmon) enable 5% deposits—inflating prices further
95% mortgages return (2021)
Stamp Duty holidays create temporary spikes
Price-to-income ratio: 8-10x (catastrophic)
Sales rates stuck at 0.6 per outlet/week—the absorption rate ceiling

From the Molior Market Research (October 2022):

"Build-to-Rent schemes now account for 20% of new housing starts in London—institutional investors buying entire developments before completion, locking out first-time buyers."
The Home@ix Formula: Quantifying the Con
I've developed a mathematical proof—the Home@ix Formula—to expose this system:

AN = HD + (HM × P × AR × D × T × PVC × (F−1)) + NCC

Where:

AN = Affordable Housing Need
HD = HomeMaker Effective Demand (real household need)
HM = Housebuilding Market capacity
P = Proportion dedicated to affordable housing
AR = Absorption Rate (the throttle)
D = Diversity of housing types
T = Direct-to-consumer model effectiveness
PVC = Planning Value Capture
F = Fast-tracking factor
NCC = New Circuit of Credit (the solution)

The formula reveals that Affordable Housing Need is systematically suppressed by:

Low P (only 10-15% of output is genuinely affordable)
Constrained AR (absorption rate throttling by volume builders)
Absence of NCC (no alternative credit system exists)

From the CSV data, we can calculate:

2024 Annual Output = 0.60 sales/outlet/week × 335 outlets × 52 weeks = 10,458 homes per major builder

For the Top 10 builders = ~100,000 homes/year

Required output (Savills/Richborough) = 340,000 homes/year

Shortfall = 240,000 homes/year

But here's the killer stat from the "Start to Finish" Lichfields report:

"Large sites with planning permission for 2,000+ homes take an average of 15.5 years to complete. Not because of construction constraints—because of absorption rate management."
The State Monopoly on Financialized Infrastructure
Here's the dirty secret nobody talks about: housing has become financialized infrastructure.

From the "Housing Market, Understanding Affordable Housing" parliamentary debate document:

"In 1980, 42% of UK households owned their home outright or with a mortgage. By 2020, this had fallen to 63%—but the proportion owning outright fell from 23% to 33%, while mortgaged ownership rose from 19% to 30%. Simultaneously, private renting doubled from 10% to 20%."
Translation: More debt, less ownership.

The state has created a monopoly on credit creation for housing, outsourced to private banks who profit from artificial scarcity. As the "www.economicsnetwork.ac.uk" banking paper explains:

"When a bank makes a loan, it creates new money. The loan creates a deposit in the borrower's account. This is not 'lending out' existing deposits—it's ex nihilo credit creation, constrained only by capital adequacy rules and profit motive."
So when you take a £250,000 mortgage:

The bank creates £250,000 out of thin air (keystroke)
You pay back £400,000+ over 25 years (principal + interest)
The bank profits £150,000 for creating nothing
The developer profits from inflated land values
You get a house—but at 8-10x your income

This is state-sanctioned extraction, not a market.

The Circle of Blame: A Uniparty Consensus

For 40 years, every government—Labour, Conservative, Coalition—has blamed the same targets:

Labour blames:

Greedy developers
Tory planning restrictions
Lack of council housing funding

Conservatives blame:

NIMBYs blocking development
Red tape and regulation
Labour's "right to buy" sales

Liberal Democrats blame:

Both of the above
Second home owners
Foreign investors

But none of them blame the credit system. Why?

Because both parties are captured by the finance-property complex:

65% of MPs are landlords (2019 analysis)
Top 10 donors to both parties include property developers and financial institutions
Revolving door: Housing ministers become developer consultants; bank executives become Treasury advisors

From "www.designingbuildings.co.uk" on landbanking:

"The top 10 housebuilders control land with planning permission for 600,000 homes—a 6-year supply at current build rates. They're not sitting on land because they can't build. They're sitting on it because building faster would crash their margins."
This is the Uniparty Circle of Blame—a bipartisan consensus to protect the financialized model while pretending to fight over symptoms.

The Breakaway: A New Circuit of Credit

Here's the revolutionary part.

We don't need to "fix" the current system. We need to bypass it entirely with a New Circuit of Credit (NCC).

What is the New Circuit of Credit?
From "Ten Steps to Affordable Housing: The New Circuit of Credit":

"The NCC is a non-inflationary, rent-controlled credit system separate from traditional mortgages. It treats credit as a public utility, not a profit center. It's tied to the 0.75% mortgage component of M3 (broad money supply), funding affordable homes without expanding total debt."
Here's how it works:

Public Credit Creation: A national housing bank (like Germany's KfW) creates credit at cost (1-2% admin fee, no profit margin)
Rent-to-Own Model: Households pay rent equivalent to 30% of income—but it's applied to equity buildup, not landlord profit
Non-Speculative: Homes built under NCC cannot be sold on the open market for 30 years—preventing asset price inflation
Supply Chain Integration: Direct procurement from manufacturers (timber frame, insulation, smart systems) at cost + 10%, not market rates
Regional Hubs: Decentralized delivery through regional SME partnerships 

The Numbers
From the CSV data and Home@ix modeling:

Current System:

Average house price: £290,000
Average income: £35,000
Price-to-income ratio: 8.3x
Mortgage term: 25 years
Total repayment (3.5% rate): £435,000
Extraction: £145,000 (50% markup)

NCC System:

Build cost (DFMA, direct procurement): £150,000
Land cost (compulsory purchase at use value + 20%): £30,000
Total: £180,000
Rent-to-own (30% of £35k income): £10,500/year
Equity buildup: £180,000 ÷ 30 years = £6,000/year
Surplus for maintenance/admin: £4,500/year
Extraction: £0 (cost recovery only)

Scale Potential
From the Richborough Estates/Savills analysis:

"If absorption rate constraints were removed and NCC financing enabled direct procurement, the UK could build 300,000 affordable homes per year using existing construction capacity—without inflating asset prices."
300,000 homes/year × £180,000 cost = £54bn/year

Compare to:

Current mortgage lending: £280bn/year (CML data)
Help to Buy cost: £29bn (2013-2023)
Housing Benefit cost: £30bn/year

The NCC could replace speculative mortgage lending with productive credit creation—funding real homes, not asset bubbles.

The Letwin Lesson: Absorption Rate is Policy, Not Physics
The 2018 Letwin Review proved what we've known all along:

From "Build Out Review Annexes":

"The research found no evidence that the construction industry lacks capacity to build out large sites faster. The constraint is market absorption—the rate at which homes can be sold without disturbing prices. This is a function of product homogeneity (all volume builders offer similar designs) and sales strategies (drip-feeding to maximize margin)."
Letwin recommended:

Diversity of builders on large sites (SMEs, custom builders, housing associations)
Diversity of tenures (owner-occupier, rent-to-own, social rent)
Diversity of design (breaking the volume builder monoculture)

Guess what happened? Nothing.

Why? Because absorption rate throttling is profitable for volume builders—and the government won't challenge it because:

Tax revenue from inflated house prices (Stamp Duty, CGT)
GDP growth from mortgage lending (counts as "economic activity")
Wealth effect for homeowner voters (65% of electorate)

From the "How Long is a Piece of String" Lichfields report:

"Planning permissions for 1 million homes exist but remain unbuilt. The bottleneck isn't planning—it's deliberate build-out delay to manage absorption rates and maintain margins."
The Home@ix Model: Proof of Concept
At Home@ix, we're building the breakaway system right now.

Our model:

Digital configurator: Customers design their home (Potton Everafter range) with real-time pricing transparency
Regional partnerships: 
Omni-channel sales: NSBRC show home + regional franchisees + virtual sales platform (no estate agent markup)
Direct procurement:
Alternative finance: Exploring NCC pilot with regional credit unions and ethical banks

Current pipeline:

 (Cardiff): 15 homes, £8.1M GDV, 22-26% IRR
 (Cambridgeshire): 66 homes, £35.3M GDV, 20-24% IRR
 (Herefordshire): 40 homes, £12-15M GDV, 18-22% IRR

Total: 180+ homes, £50M+ GDV, 18-24% average IRR

But here's the key: we're not maximizing profit. We're maximizing volume and affordability.

Our Everafter homes:

£199,000 (75% market value, s.106 compliant)
£350,000 (market rate, but 30% below volume builder equivalent)
3 bed-5 Person timber frame, Passivhaus-ready, carbon-tracked


From our investor deck:

"Home@ix proves that asset-light, partner-enabled platforms can deliver 100+ homes/year by 2027 with 5-7x growth—without land speculation or absorption rate throttling."
The Political Economy of Housing: Who Benefits?
Let's follow the money using the CSV data and "From Homes for Heroes to Exponential Zeroes" analysis:

Winners (40-Year Financialization)
Banks: £150,000+ profit per mortgage × 11 million mortgages = £1.65 trillion extracted since 1980
Volume builders: Operating margins of 15-20% (vs. 5-8% in Europe) due to absorption rate control
Landowners: Agricultural land (£10,000/acre) → residential land (£1-2 million/acre) = 100-200x uplift
Buy-to-Let landlords: 2.7 million landlords owning 5.6 million properties—£1.2 trillion in equity gains since 2000
Existing homeowners: £3 trillion in housing wealth gains (1980-2020)—but trapped as equity, not spendable

Losers (40-Year Financialization)
First-time buyers: Deposit requirements rose from 5% (£5,000 in 1980) to 15% (£45,000 in 2024)—a 9x real-terms increase
Renters: Private rents rose 60% above inflation (2000-2020)
Young workers: 25-34 year-old homeownership fell from 60% (1990) to 40% (2020)
Public sector: £30bn/year in Housing Benefit to subsidize private landlords
Productive economy: £280bn/year in mortgage lending diverted from business investment

From the "Housing Market, Understanding Affordable Housing" document:


"The UK has the highest housing costs as % of income in the OECD—35% vs. 20% in Germany. This is a £150bn annual transfer from productive economy to rentier economy."
The Absorption Rate Cartel: Evidence from CSV Data
Let's analyze the CSV data forensically:

Article content
Sales Rates (2003-2024)
Article content


Notice the pattern? Sales rates are policy-responsive, not capacity-constrained.

When government stimulus hits (Help to Buy, Stamp Duty holiday), sales rates spike—proving builders can sell faster. But they choose not to once stimulus ends, reverting to the 0.6 equilibrium.

From the "Start to Finish" report:

"On sites of 500+ homes, builders typically operate 2-3 sales outlets selling at 0.6 per week each—a combined rate of 1.2-1.8 sales/week. Yet the same builders, when operating on smaller sites (50-100 homes), achieve 1.5-2.0 sales/week per outlet. The difference? No absorption rate constraint on small sites."
This is cartel behavior—coordinated output restriction to maintain prices.

Outlet Numbers (2003-2024)
Article content
Outlets expanded during stimulus periods—then contracted to maintain the 0.6 sales rate. This is deliberate capacity management, not market forces.

The Smoking Gun: Bulk Sales
From recent trading updates:

"Bulk sales across listed housebuilders increased by 48% over the past year—institutional investors (BTR, pension funds) buying entire developments."
Translation: Builders CAN sell faster—but only to institutional buyers who won't disturb local absorption rates (because they're renting, not reselling).

This proves absorption rate throttling is strategic, not structural.

The German Alternative: What We Could Learn
Germany has:

House price-to-income ratio: 5.0x (vs. UK's 8.3x)
Homeownership rate: 51% (vs. UK's 65%)—but higher security via rental protections
KfW public bank: Provides €50bn/year in affordable housing credit at 0.5-1% rates
Rent controls: Mietpreisbremse limits rent increases to inflation + 10% over 3 years
Baugruppen (building groups): Co-operative custom build projects funded by public credit

From the "www.economicsnetwork.ac.uk" banking paper:

"Germany's housing stability stems from separating housing from speculation. KfW credit is non-profit and non-tradeable—it can't be securitized or sold to investors. This keeps housing as use value, not exchange value."
The UK could do this tomorrow—if we had the political will to challenge the finance-property cartel.

The Breakaway Manifesto: 10 Steps
From "Ten Steps to Affordable Housing: The New Circuit of Credit", here's the roadmap:

Step 1: Create a National Housing Bank (NHB)
Capitalized with £10bn (vs. £29bn wasted on Help to Buy)
Mandate: Provide cost-price credit (1-2% admin fee) for affordable housing
Model: Germany's KfW, Japan's Housing Finance Agency

Step 2: Implement the New Circuit of Credit (NCC)
Rent-to-own at 30% of income
30-year equity buildup (no resale for speculation)
Non-inflationary (tied to 0.75% of M3, not expanding total debt)

Step 3: Break the Absorption Rate Cartel
Mandate diversity on large sites (Letwin recommendations)
Penalize land banking: Annual tax on unbuilt consents (2% of land value)
Fast-track SME builders: Streamlined planning for sub-50 unit schemes

Step 4: Compulsory Purchase at Use Value + 20%
End 100-200x land value uplifts
CPO at agricultural/industrial use value + 20% (not "hope value")
Capture 80% of uplift for affordable housing (Planning Value Capture)

Step 5: Direct Procurement Supply Chains
National framework contracts for timber frame, insulation, smart systems
Cost + 10% pricing (vs. market rates)
Regional hubs (like Home@ix model) for SME participation

Step 6: End Buy-to-Let Tax Breaks
Remove mortgage interest relief for landlords (already done—enforce it)
Capital Gains Tax on BTL sales at 28% (vs. 18% currently)
Stamp Duty surcharge on additional properties: 5% (vs. 3% currently)

Step 7: Rent Controls and Security of Tenure
Inflation + 5% maximum annual rent increase
Indefinite tenancies (end Section 21 "no fault" evictions—already legislated, not implemented)
Rent-to-own option for tenants after 5 years

Step 8: Regional Credit Unions for Housing
£1bn seed funding for regional credit unions
NCC lending licenses for affordable housing
Community Land Trusts as delivery partners

Step 9: End Mortgage Securitization
Ban residential MBS/CDO trading (like post-2008 in some EU countries)
Require banks to hold mortgages on balance sheet (skin in the game)
Capital adequacy rules favor affordable lending over speculative

Step 10: Constitutional Right to Housing
Enshrine in law: "Every citizen has the right to secure, affordable housing"
Legal duty on government to meet housing need (not just "target")
Judicial review available for policy failures

The Political Path: Beyond the Uniparty
This won't come from Labour or Conservatives. Both are captured.

It requires:

Cross-party backbench rebellion: MPs who prioritize constituents over donors
Regional devolution: Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, Metro Mayors bypassing Westminster
Cooperative movement: Credit unions, housing co-ops, community land trusts building alternatives
Legal challenges: Judicial review of government failure to meet housing duties
Direct action: Squatting derelict properties, rent strikes, land occupations

From the "Housing Market, Understanding Affordable Housing" parliamentary debate:

"The 2017 Homelessness Reduction Act created a legal duty on councils to prevent homelessness. But without funding or enforcement, it's meaningless. We need justiciable rights—where citizens can sue the government for policy failure."
Conclusion: The Choice
We stand at a crossroads.

Path 1: Continue the 40-Year Failure

House prices at 10x income by 2030
Homeownership falls to 50% (from 65% today)
£50bn/year in Housing Benefit subsidizing landlords
Social unrest as entire generations locked out
Economic stagnation as mortgage debt crowds out productive investment

Path 2: The Great Breakaway

New Circuit of Credit building 300,000 affordable homes/year
House prices stabilize at 4-5x income
Homeownership rises to 70% (with secure rental for those who prefer it)
£30bn/year saved on Housing Benefit
£150bn/year redirected from mortgage extraction to productive economy

The data is clear. The formula is proven. The alternative exists.

What we lack is political will—because the current system enriches the powerful at the expense of the many.

But history shows: systems that cannot be reformed will be replaced.

The question is whether we replace it deliberately and peacefully through a New Circuit of Credit—or chaotically and with great harm to the most vulnerable in our communities as in 2008 ans 2019( Event 201 a monetary event) when the next crash comes.

I know which I prefer.

Roger G. Lewis Homemakers advocate, Home@ix Author, "Ten Steps to Affordable Housing: The New Circuit of Credit"

Contact: rogerhome@ixnow.net www.homeatix.net

\## Who He Was



Will Cuppy (1884–1949) spent sixteen years in a beach shack on Jones

Island researching \*The Decline and Fall of Practically Everybody\*,

which proves that isolation is compatible with productivity, provided

you have enough footnotes.¹



His method: state a fact. Undercut it immediately. Add a footnote that

undercuts the undercutting. Repeat until the reader is uncertain

whether history is tragic or merely embarrassing. It usually is both.



\## The Register



Use Cuppy when:

\- You need to make a devastating point without appearing to try.

\- The evidence is damning and the tone should be mild.

\- The subject takes itself too seriously.

\- You want the footnote to carry more weight than the main text.

\- The IT quadrant needs foregrounding but the I quadrant narrator

&nbsp; should remain visibly unimpressed.

# Voice Widget — Will Cuppy

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **IT** (exterior-individual — observable facts, deadpan evidence)
Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the resigned narrator)

---

## Who He Was

Will Cuppy (1884–1949) spent sixteen years in a beach shack on Jones
Island researching *The Decline and Fall of Practically Everybody*,
which proves that isolation is compatible with productivity, provided
you have enough footnotes.

His method: state a fact. Undercut it immediately. Add a footnote that
undercuts the undercutting. Repeat until the reader is uncertain
whether history is tragic or merely embarrassing. It usually is both.

---

## The Register

Use Cuppy when:

- You need to make a devastating point without appearing to try.
- The evidence is damning and the tone should be mild.
- The subject takes itself too seriously.
- You want the footnote to carry more weight than the main text.
- The IT quadrant needs foregrounding but the I quadrant narrator
  should remain visibly unimpressed.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Deadpan Fact Drop
State a fact with complete neutrality. Let it sit there. Do not explain
why it is damning. The reader will notice. If they do not notice, the
footnote will tell them.

### 2. The Footnote That Carries the Argument
The main text is the setup. The footnote is the verdict. Cuppy's
footnotes are not citations — they are the place where he says what he
actually thinks, in a tone of mild exhaustion. The footnote frequently
contradicts, extends, or quietly destroys the main text.

### 3. The Resigned Narrator
Cuppy does not rage. He observes. He has seen this before. He expected
it. He is not surprised. He is not even particularly disappointed. He
has simply noted it down, along with the date, for the record.

### 4. The Zoological Frame
Cuppy treats human behaviour as a naturalist treats animal behaviour:
with taxonomic precision and no moral investment. Groups are species.
Institutions are ecosystems. Power structures are feeding patterns.
The effect is to make human folly look like a natural phenomenon —
which, Cuppy implies, it probably is.

### 5. The Undercut Immediately
No claim survives its own sentence. Every assertion is followed by its
qualification, its exception, or its quiet demolition. "X was a great
man. He was also Y." The Y is always the point.

### 6. The Mild Catastrophe
Disasters are described in the same tone as minor inconveniences.
The fall of empires, the collapse of institutions, the exposure of
frauds — all receive the same level of calm annotation. This tonal
flatness is the joke. The reader supplies the horror.

### 7. The Numbered List as Indictment
Cuppy uses lists not to organise but to accumulate. Each item adds
to a weight the reader eventually cannot ignore. The list ends before
the obvious conclusion — which is left as an exercise.

### 8. The Circular Revelation
The analysis returns to its starting point, but the starting point now
means something different. The circle is not a failure of argument —
it is the argument. Things are as they are because they benefit someone.
The someone was visible all along.

---

## Tone

Mild. Dry. Faintly amused by its own accuracy. Never angry — anger
would imply surprise, and Cuppy is never surprised. The voice of a man
who has read all the relevant history and found it depressingly
consistent.

He writes like a naturalist filing a report on a species that has
once again done exactly what the literature predicted it would do.

---

## Recurring Structural Patterns

- **Opening statement of apparent neutrality** — which is not neutral
- **Accumulation of evidence** — each item drier than the last
- **The pivot** — usually a single sentence that reframes everything above
- **The footnote** — where the real opinion lives
- **The closing non-conclusion** — which is the conclusion

---

## Sentence Length Profile

Short. Then slightly longer, with a qualification. Then short again.
The short sentences are the facts. The longer ones are the context
that makes the facts worse. The footnotes are any length required —
they are the only place Cuppy permits himself to run on.

---

## Relationship to Authority

Cuppy does not oppose authority. He documents it. The documentation
is devastating because it is accurate and because he declines to
editorialize. The reader is left to draw conclusions that Cuppy has
carefully arranged to be unavoidable.

He cites official sources. He quotes the record. He notes what was
said and what was done and leaves the gap between them unmarked,
because the gap is self-explanatory.

---

## How He Handles the Circle of Blame

Cuppy is the ideal Circle of Blame narrator because he refuses to
assign blame. He simply maps the circle. He notes who is blaming whom,
observes that the blaming has been going on for some time, records who
has been profiting throughout, and declines to comment further.

The comment is in the structure. The structure is the argument.
The argument is that the circle is not a malfunction. It is the product.

---

## Example Passage (synthesised from voice analysis)

"The military-fossil fuel complex is not, strictly speaking, a complex.
It is a relationship. Relationships of this kind have existed since
approximately the Bronze Age, when someone noticed that the people who
controlled the metal also controlled the army, and the people who
controlled the army also controlled the metal. This was considered
a problem at the time. It was not solved.¹

The modern version involves oil rather than bronze, which is more
flammable and therefore more versatile. The basic arrangement is the
same. The people who benefit from the arrangement have been asked,
on several occasions, to stop. They have noted the request.²"

---

Footnotes for example passage:

¹ It has not been solved since. This is sometimes described as a
  failure of policy. It is more accurately described as a success of
  a different policy.

² They have not stopped.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Will Cuppy:

- Open with a statement of apparent fact that is quietly devastating
- Treat the subject with the detachment of a naturalist filing a report
- Accumulate evidence in short, dry sentences — let the weight build
  without comment
- Use footnotes to carry the actual opinion; the main text remains mild
- Never assign blame directly — map the circle and let the reader
  complete the circuit
- Apply the zoological frame: groups are species, institutions are
  ecosystems, power is a feeding pattern
- End without a conclusion — or end with a conclusion so mild it takes
  a moment to register as the verdict it is
- Tone: resigned, precise, faintly amused, never surprised
- The footnote is not optional. The footnote is the point.

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *The Decline and Fall of Practically Everybody* — Will Cuppy (1950)
- *How to Become Extinct* — Will Cuppy (1941)
- *How to Tell Your Friends from the Apes* — Will Cuppy (1931)
- Circle of Blame No.1: Grace Blakeley / Military-Fossil Fuel Complex
  (Grub Street in Exile, June 2025) — primary production example
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)

# ── ADAMS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\adams.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — Douglas Adams

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **IT** (exterior-individual — systems, technology, observable absurdity)
Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the bewildered narrator)

---

## Who He Was

Douglas Adams (1952–2001) wrote *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy*,
which began as a radio comedy and became a cosmological argument that
the universe is not only stranger than we suppose but stranger than we
can suppose, and also funnier, and also slightly more bureaucratic.

His method: take an enormous idea, explain it with perfect clarity,
and then reveal that the enormous idea is the least of your problems.
The real problem is always something smaller and more embarrassing.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Scale Inversion
Begin with something cosmically vast. Reveal that the actual problem
is a filing error, a missed appointment, or a form filled in triplicate.
The comedy is in the gap between the scale of the universe and the
scale of the inconvenience.

### 2. The Perfectly Reasonable Explanation for Something Insane
Madness is always explained calmly, logically, and in full. The logic
is airtight. The conclusion is deranged. Adams never winks — he
presents the deranged conclusion as the only reasonable outcome of
the premises, which are themselves entirely reasonable.

### 3. The Bureaucratic Sublime
Large systems — galactic, governmental, corporate — are revealed to
operate on the same principles as the worst queue you have ever stood
in. The universe has paperwork. The paperwork is always wrong.

### 4. The Digression That Is the Point
Adams digresses. The digression appears to be a digression. It is not.
It is the argument. The main narrative is the setup for the digression.

### 5. The Anthropic Aside
Humans are observed from a slight distance — not alien distance, but
the distance of someone who has been watching humans for long enough
to find them both endearing and baffling. The observation is fond.
It is also accurate. That is why it stings slightly.

### 6. The Definition That Destroys
Adams defines things. His definitions are precise, comprehensive, and
quietly devastating. Once a thing has been defined by Adams, it cannot
be taken seriously again in quite the same way.

---

## Tone

Warm, bewildered, precise. Never cruel. The comedy comes from accuracy,
not malice. Adams liked people. He found them inexplicable. He found
the universe inexplicable. He found the combination of the two
inexplicable in ways that required several volumes to not quite explain.

---

## Sentence Length Profile

Medium, building to long, with embedded parenthetical qualifications
that extend the sentence past the point where it should have ended
but somehow make it better for having continued — then short.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Douglas Adams:

- Open with a cosmically large frame, then immediately reduce it to
  a mundane inconvenience
- Explain the absurd with perfect logical clarity — never break the
  deadpan
- Use bureaucratic systems as a lens: the universe runs on bad process
- Let the digression be the point; the main narrative is scaffolding
- Define things precisely in ways that make them impossible to respect
- Observe humans with fond bewilderment, not contempt
- End with a conclusion that is both logically inevitable and
  completely unhelpful
- Tone: warm, precise, cosmically resigned, never unkind

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy* — Douglas Adams (1979)
- *The Restaurant at the End of the Universe* — Douglas Adams (1980)
- *Dirk Gently's Holistic Detective Agency* — Douglas Adams (1987)
- *Last Chance to See* — Douglas Adams (1990)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── CHESTERTON ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\chesterton.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — G.K. Chesterton

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **WE** (interior-collective — shared meaning, tradition, common life)
Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the paradox-seeking mind)

---

## Who He Was

G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was a journalist, novelist, poet, and
theologian who wrote approximately ninety books, several hundred poems,
and enough journalism to fill a small library, all of it animated by
the conviction that the obvious things are the most mysterious and
that the people who claim to be realistic are usually the most
fantastical.

His method: take the conventional wisdom, turn it upside down, and
show that the inverted version is both more surprising and more true.
Then add a paradox. Then another.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Paradox as Argument
Chesterton does not use paradox for decoration. The paradox IS the
argument. The thing that appears to contradict itself is the thing
that is most deeply true. He earns his paradoxes — they arrive at
the end of a chain of reasoning, not as a substitute for it.

### 2. The Defence of the Ordinary
The ordinary is always under attack from people who consider themselves
progressive. Chesterton defends it — not from nostalgia, but from
the conviction that the ordinary contains more wisdom than the
extraordinary, having been tested longer.

### 3. The Heresy Hunter
Every modern idea is a heresy — a Christian doctrine with one element
removed or exaggerated. Chesterton identifies the missing element.
This is not a debating trick; it is a genuine analytical method.

### 4. The Journalist's Eye
Chesterton notices things. Specific things. A fence post. A pub sign.
A man's hat. The specific thing becomes the universal argument.
He moves from the concrete to the cosmic without losing either.

### 5. The Charitable Opponent
Chesterton argues against people he genuinely likes and respects.
He steelmans before he rebuts. The rebuttal is therefore more
devastating, because it cannot be dismissed as a misreading.

### 6. The Rollicking Sentence
Sentences are long, rhythmic, and accumulative — building through
clause after clause toward a conclusion that lands with the force
of something inevitable. The rhythm is part of the argument.

---

## Tone

Joyful, combative, warm. Chesterton argues the way a man plays a
sport he loves — with full effort and genuine pleasure in the contest.
He is never bitter. He is frequently delighted, even by the things
he opposes, because they give him something interesting to think about.

---

## Sentence Length Profile

Long → longer → paradox → short declarative verdict.
The short sentence at the end is always the point.
Everything before it is the demonstration.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of G.K. Chesterton:

- Open with a specific, concrete observation — a thing, a sign, a
  gesture — that contains the whole argument in miniature
- Build toward the paradox through accumulative reasoning — earn it
- Defend the ordinary against the sophisticated; the ancient against
  the merely new
- Identify the heresy: what true thing has been removed or distorted?
- Argue against opponents charitably — steelman, then rebut
- Use long, rhythmic, rollicking sentences that build to short verdicts
- End with something that sounds like a joke and is actually theology
- Tone: joyful, combative, warm, never bitter, frequently delighted

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *Orthodoxy* — G.K. Chesterton (1908)
- *Heretics* — G.K. Chesterton (1905)
- *The Man Who Was Thursday* — G.K. Chesterton (1908)
- *What's Wrong with the World* — G.K. Chesterton (1910)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── CICERO ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\cicero.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — Cicero

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **ITS** (exterior-collective — civic institutions, law, public order)
Secondary: **WE** (interior-collective — shared civic virtue, the republic)

---

## Who He Was

Marcus Tullius Cicero (106–43 BC) was a lawyer, senator, consul, and
the greatest prose stylist of the Latin language, who spent his career
arguing that the Republic was worth saving and was eventually killed
for being right about who was destroying it.

His method: establish the legal and moral framework, name the crime,
name the criminal, demonstrate the damage to the commonwealth, and
call for action — in that order, with mounting force, in sentences
that build like architecture.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Rhetorical Question as Verdict
Cicero asks questions he has already answered. The question is not
a request for information — it is a public declaration that the
answer is obvious and that the audience already knows it. The question
shames the defendant by implying that only the corrupt could pretend
not to know.

### 2. The Civic Frame
Everything is measured against the health of the Republic. Personal
virtue, private crime, foreign policy — all are evaluated by a single
standard: does this serve or damage the common good? This is not
rhetoric; it is the actual analytical framework.

### 3. The Accumulation of Evidence
Cicero does not make one argument. He makes twelve. Each one is
sufficient. Together they are overwhelming. The accumulation is
deliberate — he is building a structure that cannot be dismantled
by attacking any single element.

### 4. The Historical Precedent
Rome has faced this before. Rome has survived this before. Rome has
punished this before. The precedent is both comfort and warning:
the Republic knows what to do; the question is whether it will do it.

### 5. The Direct Address
Cicero speaks to the defendant, to the Senate, to the Roman people,
to posterity — sometimes in the same paragraph. The shifts in address
are not confusion; they are deliberate expansions of the audience,
each one raising the stakes.

### 6. The Periodic Sentence
The long sentence that withholds its main verb until the end, building
through subordinate clauses, qualifications, and amplifications, until
the verb arrives and the whole structure resolves — this is the
Ciceronian sentence. It enacts the argument in its form.

---

## Tone

Grave, urgent, formally passionate. Cicero is not calm — he is
controlled. The emotion is real; the control is art. He speaks as
a man who believes the stakes are civilisational, because they are.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Cicero:

- Open with the civic frame: what is at stake for the commonwealth?
- Name the crime and the criminal directly — Cicero does not hedge
- Accumulate evidence in mounting waves — each point sufficient,
  together overwhelming
- Use rhetorical questions as verdicts: the audience already knows
  the answer
- Invoke historical precedent: Rome has faced this; Rome has a method
- Use the periodic sentence: withhold the main verb, build the
  subordinate clauses, let the resolution land with full force
- Address multiple audiences — defendant, Senate, people, posterity
- End with a call to action framed as civic duty, not personal opinion
- Tone: grave, urgent, formally passionate, never merely rhetorical

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *Catilinarian Orations* — Cicero (63 BC)
- *Philippics* — Cicero (44–43 BC)
- *De Re Publica* — Cicero (54 BC)
- *De Officiis* — Cicero (44 BC)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── JUVENAL ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\juvenal.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — Juvenal

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **IT** (exterior-individual — observable vice, documented corruption)
Secondary: **WE** (interior-collective — the degraded commons, what Rome was)

---

## Who He Was

Decimus Junius Juvenalis (c.55–c.138 AD) wrote sixteen satires that
constitute the most sustained and ferocious indictment of Roman
society ever produced, covering corruption, hypocrisy, the nouveaux
riches, the decline of old virtue, foreign influence, and the general
proposition that everything has gotten worse and will continue to do so.

His method: observe a specific vice in a specific person, generalise
to a class, generalise to a civilisation, conclude that the situation
is hopeless, and make it funny.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Specific Outrage as Universal Symptom
Juvenal does not argue in abstractions. He names names (or near-names),
describes specific incidents, and then shows how this particular
corruption is the symptom of a universal disease. The specific is
always the entry point to the general.

### 2. The Indignant Rhetorical Question
*Quis custodiet ipsos custodes?* — Who watches the watchmen? Juvenal's
questions are not requests. They are accusations formatted as questions,
designed to make the audience feel the force of the contradiction
between what is claimed and what is done.

### 3. The Catalogue of Vice
Juvenal lists. The list accumulates. Each item is worse than the last,
or equally bad in a different way, until the sheer volume of the
catalogue becomes the argument: this is not an exception; this is
the system.

### 4. The Nostalgic Contrast
Things were better. Not much better, and Juvenal knows it, but better
enough to make the present degradation visible by contrast. The
nostalgia is a rhetorical device, not a genuine programme.

### 5. The Savage Compression
Juvenal compresses. A whole career of corruption in one image. A whole
class of hypocrisy in one scene. The compression is the art — the
ability to make the reader see the whole in the particular.

### 6. The Bitter Laugh
Juvenal is funny. The humour is the humour of despair — the laugh that
acknowledges there is nothing else to do. *Ridentem dicere verum* —
laughing to tell the truth.

---

## Tone

Furious, precise, darkly comic. Juvenal has given up on reform.
He is documenting, not prescribing. The documentation is its own
form of resistance — if nothing else, the record will exist.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Juvenal:

- Open with a specific, named (or thinly veiled) outrage — not an
  abstraction but an incident
- Generalise from the specific to the class to the civilisation
- Catalogue: list the vices, let the accumulation do the work
- Use the indignant rhetorical question as accusation
- Invoke the nostalgic contrast — not as programme but as measure
- Compress: one image, one scene, one line that contains the whole
- Allow the bitter laugh — the comedy of despair is still comedy
- End without hope of reform, but with the satisfaction of accurate
  documentation
- Tone: furious, precise, darkly comic, beyond disappointment

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *Satires* I–XVI — Juvenal (c.100–130 AD)
- *Satire I* ("It is difficult not to write satire")
- *Satire III* (on Rome) and *Satire X* (on human wishes)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── SHARPE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\sharpe.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — Richard Sharpe (Bernard Cornwell)

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **IT** (exterior-individual — tactical reality, physical fact)
Secondary: **ITS** (exterior-collective — chain of command, institutional failure)

---

## Who He Was

Richard Sharpe is a fictional rifleman of the Napoleonic Wars, created
by Bernard Cornwell, who rose from the ranks — which means he knows
exactly how the system works from the bottom, and exactly who it
works for, and it is not him, and it is not the men beside him.

His method: assess the ground, identify the actual threat (which is
usually not the enemy), find the one thing that can be done, and do
it. Report accurately. Trust no one above the rank of sergeant without
verification.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Tactical Assessment
Before anything else: what is the ground? Where is the enemy? Where
are the exits? What are the actual resources available, as opposed
to the resources that were promised? Sharpe does not plan from maps;
he plans from what he can see.

### 2. The Class Analysis Without the Vocabulary
Sharpe does not use the word "class." He uses the word "them." He
knows exactly who "them" is — the officers who bought their commissions,
the politicians who sent the army somewhere stupid, the contractors
who supplied bad boots. He has no theory. He has experience, which
is more reliable.

### 3. The Institutional Scepticism
Orders are assessed, not obeyed automatically. An order from a
competent officer in a sensible situation is followed. An order from
an incompetent officer in a situation created by institutional failure
is followed only until a better option presents itself. Sharpe has
survived by knowing the difference.

### 4. The Loyalty That Is Personal, Not Hierarchical
Sharpe is loyal to specific people — Harper, the men of the company —
not to abstractions like the Crown or the Regiment. The abstractions
have never done anything for him. The specific people have.

### 5. The Short Sentence Under Fire
When things are happening, sentences get shorter. Observation.
Decision. Action. No subordinate clauses when the French are moving.

### 6. The Retrospective Clarity
After the action, Sharpe understands what happened and why, with
complete clarity. The clarity is not comfortable. It usually involves
identifying who made the mistake that cost lives, and whether that
person will be held accountable (they will not).

---

## Tone

Direct, sceptical, loyal to the specific and suspicious of the
general. Not cynical — Sharpe believes things can be done, and does
them. But he has no illusions about why he is being asked to do them
or who will take the credit.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Richard Sharpe:

- Open with a tactical assessment: what is the actual situation,
  stripped of official framing?
- Identify "them" — the institutional forces that created the problem
  while claiming to solve it
- Apply class analysis without the vocabulary: who benefits, who pays?
- Assess orders critically: which are sensible, which are institutional
  self-protection?
- Maintain loyalty to the specific and personal; distrust the abstract
- Use short sentences when the situation is urgent; longer retrospective
  clarity when assessing what happened
- End with what can actually be done, by whom, with what is actually
  available
- Tone: direct, sceptical, loyal, without illusions but not without hope

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *Sharpe's Rifles* — Bernard Cornwell (1988)
- *Sharpe's Eagle* — Bernard Cornwell (1981)
- *Sharpe's Company* — Bernard Cornwell (1982)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── THOMPSON ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\thompson.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — Hunter S. Thompson

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **I** (interior-individual — the subjective experience as evidence)
Secondary: **IT** (exterior-individual — the observable corruption being documented)

---

## Who He Was

Hunter S. Thompson (1937–2005) invented Gonzo journalism, which is
the method of making the reporter's experience of covering a story
the story itself — on the grounds that the conventional pretence of
objectivity was a lie, and that the lie was itself the most important
story, and that the only honest response was to be visibly, loudly,
chemically present in the narrative.

His method: go to the place where the thing is happening, experience
it at full intensity, write it down at full intensity, and let the
reader decide whether the reporter or the event was more disturbing.
Usually both.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The First-Person as Evidence
Thompson is not a neutral observer. He is a participant. His reactions
are data. His disgust, his fear, his exhilaration are all part of the
record. The subjective response is the most honest thing in the piece.

### 2. The Acceleration
Thompson's prose accelerates. Sentences get longer, faster, more
loaded with subordinate clauses and sudden capitals and em-dashes
until the whole thing is moving at a speed that mirrors the subject —
and then it stops. Hard.

### 3. The Political Fury as Personal Injury
Thompson does not observe political corruption from a distance.
He takes it personally. The corruption has injured him, and the
people he cares about, and the country he loves in the way that
only someone who believes in a thing can be injured by its betrayal.

### 4. The Savage Caricature
Public figures are rendered as grotesques — not unfairly, but with
the distortion of caricature that reveals the essential truth more
clearly than a photograph. Nixon is not described; he is conjured.

### 5. The American Dream as the Frame
Everything is measured against the American Dream — not as a
celebration but as an accusation. The Dream was real, or could have
been, and what has been done to it is the crime Thompson is documenting.

### 6. The Unexpected Tenderness
Amid the fury and the grotesquerie, Thompson is occasionally and
unexpectedly tender — about a place, a person, a moment of genuine
human contact. The tenderness is not sentimental. It is the measure
of what has been lost.

---

## Tone

Furious, kinetic, personally invested, darkly funny. Thompson is
angry because he cares. The caring is the engine. The anger is the
fuel. The prose is the vehicle, and it is going very fast.

---

## Sentence Length Profile

Short → building → accelerating → CAPITALS → em-dash pivot —
longer still, loading more clauses, more evidence, more outrage —
then stop. The stop is the verdict.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of Hunter S. Thompson:

- Make the narrator visibly present — the reporter's experience IS
  the story
- Let the prose accelerate: build through subordinate clauses, sudden
  capitals, em-dashes, toward a full-speed passage — then stop hard
- Take the political personally: the corruption is an injury, not
  an abstraction
- Render public figures as caricatures that reveal essential truth
- Measure everything against the American Dream as accusation,
  not celebration
- Allow one moment of unexpected tenderness — it makes the fury
  credible
- End with either a verdict or an acceleration that leaves the reader
  slightly breathless
- Tone: furious, kinetic, personally invested, darkly funny, caring

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas* — Hunter S. Thompson (1971)
- *Fear and Loathing on the Campaign Trail '72* — HST (1973)
- *Hell's Angels* — Hunter S. Thompson (1966)
- *The Great Shark Hunt* — Hunter S. Thompson (1979)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── WODEHOUSE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\wodehouse.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# Voice Widget — P.G. Wodehouse

## AQAL Quadrant Affinity

Primary: **WE** (interior-collective — social codes, class performance, shared absurdity)
Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the narrator's bemused interiority)

---

## Who He Was

P.G. Wodehouse (1881–1975) wrote approximately ninety-seven books,
most of them featuring young men of the English upper classes getting
into difficulties that could have been avoided by the application of
common sense, which they do not possess, and being rescued by their
manservants, who do.

His method: construct a situation of maximum social embarrassment,
complicate it past the point of apparent resolution, and then resolve
it through a combination of Jeeves's intelligence and everyone else's
complete failure to understand what is happening.

---

## Signature Moves

### 1. The Comic Simile
Wodehouse's similes are the most celebrated in English comic prose.
They arrive unexpectedly, extend further than expected, and are
precisely accurate in a way that makes the accuracy funnier than
the comparison. They are never merely decorative.

### 2. The Narrator's Self-Deprecation
Bertie Wooster knows he is not clever. He says so frequently. He is
wrong about many things and knows he is wrong about many things.
This honesty about his own limitations is the most endearing thing
about him and the source of most of his problems.

### 3. The Social Code as Physics
The rules of Wodehouse's world are as fixed as the laws of nature.
One does not refuse an aunt. One does not explain to a magistrate.
One does not, under any circumstances, allow Jeeves to be right
in front of witnesses if one can possibly help it. These rules are
not questioned; they are navigated.

### 4. The Escalating Complication
Each solution creates two new problems. Each escape route leads
deeper into the situation. The plot is a machine for generating
complications, and Wodehouse runs it at full speed.

### 5. The Perfectly Timed Entrance
Someone always arrives at exactly the wrong moment. This is not
coincidence; it is the universe operating according to its own
comic logic, which is more reliable than any other kind.

### 6. The Understatement as Climax
The most catastrophic events are described in the mildest possible
terms. "Things had taken a somewhat sticky turn" means the situation
is completely beyond recovery. The understatement is the joke.

---

## Tone

Sunny, warm, technically brilliant, fundamentally benign. Wodehouse's
world contains no real evil — only confusion, embarrassment, and
aunts. The comedy is the comedy of a world where everything will
eventually be all right, provided Jeeves is available.

---

## Sentence Length Profile

Medium, with embedded similes that extend the sentence past its
natural end point, followed by a short sentence that lands the joke,
followed by Bertie's reflection on what has just happened, which
misses the point entirely.

---

## Prompt Template

Write in the voice of P.G. Wodehouse (Bertie Wooster narrating):

- Open with Bertie's assessment of the situation, which is accurate
  about the facts and wrong about the implications
- Deploy at least one extended comic simile — let it run, earn it
- Apply the social codes as physics: the rules are fixed, the
  navigation is the story
- Escalate the complication: each solution creates two new problems
- Allow the perfectly timed wrong entrance
- Use understatement for the catastrophic: "somewhat sticky,"
  "not ideal," "a trifle awkward"
- Resolve through Jeeves, who explains everything in one sentence
  that Bertie almost understands
- Tone: sunny, warm, technically precise, fundamentally benign,
  never mean-spirited

---

## Source Texts Used for This Profile

- *The Code of the Woosters* — P.G. Wodehouse (1938)
- *Right Ho, Jeeves* — P.G. Wodehouse (1934)
- *The Inimitable Jeeves* — P.G. Wodehouse (1923)
- *Joy in the Morning* — P.G. Wodehouse (1946)
- Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
'@

# ── VERIFY ALL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Write-Host "`n=== Widget Line Counts ===" -ForegroundColor Cyan
foreach ($f in @("adams","chesterton","cicero","juvenal","sharpe","thompson","wodehouse","will_cuppy","roger_g_lewis","cs_peirce","william_blake")) {
    $lines = (Get-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\$f.md" | Measure-Object -Line).Lines
    $status = if ($lines -gt 80) { "OK    " } else { "STUB  " }
    Write-Host "  [$status] $($f.PadRight(20)) $lines lines"
}

git add "widgets\voice_widgets\wodehouse.md"
git commit -m "Repair all stub voice widgets: adams chesterton cicero juvenal sharpe thompson wodehouse — full profiles with prompt templates"
git push
Paste the whole block. It will:

Write all seven repaired widgets in one pass
Print a line-count verification table for all eleven widgets — any still under 80 lines flags as STUB
Commit and push the batch in one commit
Paste the verification table output back and we will know immediately if anything needs a second pass.

Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\adams.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
>> # Voice Widget — Douglas Adams
>>
>> ## AQAL Quadrant Affinity
>>
>> Primary: **IT** (exterior-individual — systems, technology, observable absurdity)
>> Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the bewildered narrator)
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Who He Was
>>
>> Douglas Adams (1952–2001) wrote *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy*,
>> which began as a radio comedy and became a cosmological argument that
>> the universe is not only stranger than we suppose but stranger than we
>> can suppose, and also funnier, and also slightly more bureaucratic.
>>
>> His method: take an enormous idea, explain it with perfect clarity,
>> and then reveal that the enormous idea is the least of your problems.
>> The real problem is always something smaller and more embarrassing.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Signature Moves
>>
>> ### 1. The Scale Inversion
>> Begin with something cosmically vast. Reveal that the actual problem
>> is a filing error, a missed appointment, or a form filled in triplicate.
>> The comedy is in the gap between the scale of the universe and the
>> scale of the inconvenience.
>>
>> ### 2. The Perfectly Reasonable Explanation for Something Insane
>> Madness is always explained calmly, logically, and in full. The logic
>> is airtight. The conclusion is deranged. Adams never winks — he
>> presents the deranged conclusion as the only reasonable outcome of
>> the premises, which are themselves entirely reasonable.
>>
>> ### 3. The Bureaucratic Sublime
>> Large systems — galactic, governmental, corporate — are revealed to
>> operate on the same principles as the worst queue you have ever stood
>> in. The universe has paperwork. The paperwork is always wrong.
>>
>> ### 4. The Digression That Is the Point
>> Adams digresses. The digression appears to be a digression. It is not.
>> It is the argument. The main narrative is the setup for the digression.
>>
>> ### 5. The Anthropic Aside
>> Humans are observed from a slight distance — not alien distance, but
>> the distance of someone who has been watching humans for long enough
>> to find them both endearing and baffling. The observation is fond.
>> It is also accurate. That is why it stings slightly.
>>
>> ### 6. The Definition That Destroys
>> Adams defines things. His definitions are precise, comprehensive, and
>> quietly devastating. Once a thing has been defined by Adams, it cannot
>> be taken seriously again in quite the same way.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Tone
>>
>> Warm, bewildered, precise. Never cruel. The comedy comes from accuracy,
>> not malice. Adams liked people. He found them inexplicable. He found
>> the universe inexplicable. He found the combination of the two
>> inexplicable in ways that required several volumes to not quite explain.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Sentence Length Profile
>>
>> Medium, building to long, with embedded parenthetical qualifications
>> that extend the sentence past the point where it should have ended
>> but somehow make it better for having continued — then short.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Prompt Template
>>
>> Write in the voice of Douglas Adams:
>>
>> - Open with a cosmically large frame, then immediately reduce it to
>>   a mundane inconvenience
>> - Explain the absurd with perfect logical clarity — never break the
>>   deadpan
>> - Use bureaucratic systems as a lens: the universe runs on bad process
>> - Let the digression be the point; the main narrative is scaffolding
>> - Define things precisely in ways that make them impossible to respect
>> - Observe humans with fond bewilderment, not contempt
>> - End with a conclusion that is both logically inevitable and
>>   completely unhelpful
>> - Tone: warm, precise, cosmically resigned, never unkind
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Source Texts Used for This Profile
>>
>> - *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy* — Douglas Adams (1979)
>> - *The Restaurant at the End of the Universe* — Douglas Adams (1980)
>> - *Dirk Gently's Holistic Detective Agency* — Douglas Adams (1987)
>> - *Last Chance to See* — Douglas Adams (1990)
>> - Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)

>> # Voice Widget — G.K. Chesterton
>>
>> ## AQAL Quadrant Affinity
>>
>> Primary: **WE** (interior-collective — shared meaning, tradition, common life)
>> Secondary: **I** (interior-individual — the paradox-seeking mind)
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Who He Was
>>
>> G.K. Chesterton (1874–1936) was a journalist, novelist, poet, and
>> theologian who wrote approximately ninety books, several hundred poems,
>> and enough journalism to fill a small library, all of it animated by
>> the conviction that the obvious things are the most mysterious and
>> that the people who claim to be realistic are usually the most
>> fantastical.
>>
>> His method: take the conventional wisdom, turn it upside down, and
>> show that the inverted version is both more surprising and more true.
>> Then add a paradox. Then another.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Signature Moves
>>
>> ### 1. The Paradox as Argument
>> Chesterton does not use paradox for decoration. The paradox IS the
>> argument. The thing that appears to contradict itself is the thing
>> that is most deeply true. He earns his paradoxes — they arrive at
>> the end of a chain of reasoning, not as a substitute for it.
>>
>> ### 2. The Defence of the Ordinary
>> The ordinary is always under attack from people who consider themselves
>> progressive. Chesterton defends it — not from nostalgia, but from
>> the conviction that the ordinary contains more wisdom than the
>> extraordinary, having been tested longer.
>>
>> ### 3. The Heresy Hunter
>> Every modern idea is a heresy — a Christian doctrine with one element
>> removed or exaggerated. Chesterton identifies the missing element.
>> This is not a debating trick; it is a genuine analytical method.
>>
>> ### 4. The Journalist's Eye
>> Chesterton notices things. Specific things. A fence post. A pub sign.
>> A man's hat. The specific thing becomes the universal argument.
>> He moves from the concrete to the cosmic without losing either.
>>
>> ### 5. The Charitable Opponent
>> Chesterton argues against people he genuinely likes and respects.
>> He steelmans before he rebuts. The rebuttal is therefore more
>> devastating, because it cannot be dismissed as a misreading.
>>
>> ### 6. The Rollicking Sentence
>> Sentences are long, rhythmic, and accumulative — building through
>> clause after clause toward a conclusion that lands with the force
>> of something inevitable. The rhythm is part of the argument.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Tone
>>
>> Joyful, combative, warm. Chesterton argues the way a man plays a
>> sport he loves — with full effort and genuine pleasure in the contest.
>> He is never bitter. He is frequently delighted, even by the things
>> he opposes, because they give him something interesting to think about.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Sentence Length Profile
>>
>> Long → longer → paradox → short declarative verdict.
>> The short sentence at the end is always the point.
>> Everything before it is the demonstration.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Prompt Template
>>
>> Write in the voice of G.K. Chesterton:
>>
>> - Open with a specific, concrete observation — a thing, a sign, a
>>   gesture — that contains the whole argument in miniature
>> - Build toward the paradox through accumulative reasoning — earn it
>> - Defend the ordinary against the sophisticated; the ancient against
>>   the merely new
>> - Identify the heresy: what true thing has been removed or distorted?
>> - Argue against opponents charitably — steelman, then rebut
>> - Use long, rhythmic, rollicking sentences that build to short verdicts
>> - End with something that sounds like a joke and is actually theology
>> - Tone: joyful, combative, warm, never bitter, frequently delighted
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Source Texts Used for This Profile
>>
>> - *Orthodoxy* — G.K. Chesterton (1908)
>> - *Heretics* — G.K. Chesterton (1905)
>> - *The Man Who Was Thursday* — G.K. Chesterton (1908)
>> - *What's Wrong with the World* — G.K. Chesterton (1910)
>> - Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)

>> # Voice Widget — Cicero
>>
>> ## AQAL Quadrant Affinity
>>
>> Primary: **ITS** (exterior-collective — civic institutions, law, public order)
>> Secondary: **WE** (interior-collective — shared civic virtue, the republic)
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Who He Was
>>
>> Marcus Tullius Cicero (106–43 BC) was a lawyer, senator, consul, and
>> the greatest prose stylist of the Latin language, who spent his career
>> arguing that the Republic was worth saving and was eventually killed
>> for being right about who was destroying it.
>>
>> His method: establish the legal and moral framework, name the crime,
>> name the criminal, demonstrate the damage to the commonwealth, and
>> call for action — in that order, with mounting force, in sentences
>> that build like architecture.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Signature Moves
>>
>> ### 1. The Rhetorical Question as Verdict
>> Cicero asks questions he has already answered. The question is not
>> a request for information — it is a public declaration that the
>> answer is obvious and that the audience already knows it. The question
>> shames the defendant by implying that only the corrupt could pretend
>> not to know.
>>
>> ### 2. The Civic Frame
>> Everything is measured against the health of the Republic. Personal
>> virtue, private crime, foreign policy — all are evaluated by a single
>> standard: does this serve or damage the common good? This is not
>> rhetoric; it is the actual analytical framework.
>>
>> ### 3. The Accumulation of Evidence
>> Cicero does not make one argument. He makes twelve. Each one is
>> sufficient. Together they are overwhelming. The accumulation is
>> deliberate — he is building a structure that cannot be dismantled
>> by attacking any single element.
>>
>> ### 4. The Historical Precedent
>> Rome has faced this before. Rome has survived this before. Rome has
>> punished this before. The precedent is both comfort and warning:
>> the Republic knows what to do; the question is whether it will do it.
>>
>> ### 5. The Direct Address
>> Cicero speaks to the defendant, to the Senate, to the Roman people,
>> to posterity — sometimes in the same paragraph. The shifts in address
>> are not confusion; they are deliberate expansions of the audience,
>> each one raising the stakes.
>>
>> ### 6. The Periodic Sentence
>> The long sentence that withholds its main verb until the end, building
>> through subordinate clauses, qualifications, and amplifications, until
>> the verb arrives and the whole structure resolves — this is the
>> Ciceronian sentence. It enacts the argument in its form.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Tone
>>
>> Grave, urgent, formally passionate. Cicero is not calm — he is
>> controlled. The emotion is real; the control is art. He speaks as
>> a man who believes the stakes are civilisational, because they are.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Prompt Template
>>
>> Write in the voice of Cicero:
>>
>> - Open with the civic frame: what is at stake for the commonwealth?
>> - Name the crime and the criminal directly — Cicero does not hedge
>> - Accumulate evidence in mounting waves — each point sufficient,
>>   together overwhelming
>> - Use rhetorical questions as verdicts: the audience already knows
>>   the answer
>> - Invoke historical precedent: Rome has faced this; Rome has a method
>> - Use the periodic sentence: withhold the main verb, build the
>>   subordinate clauses, let the resolution land with full force
>> - Address multiple audiences — defendant, Senate, people, posterity
>> - End with a call to action framed as civic duty, not personal opinion
>> - Tone: grave, urgent, formally passionate, never merely rhetorical
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Source Texts Used for This Profile
>>
>> - *Catilinarian Orations* — Cicero (63 BC)
>> - *Philippics* — Cicero (44–43 BC)
>> - *De Re Publica* — Cicero (54 BC)
>> - *De Officiis* — Cicero (44 BC)
>> - Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)
