📜 Step 2 — Foster Lewis Writes the Foreword
Once the widget is committed, paste this prompt into a new session with the kit loaded:

Copy
Using widgets/voice_widgets/foster_lewis.md, write a foreword
in Foster Lewis's voice for Roger G. Lewis's collected works.

Model it on the introductory tradition of the Pope/Cassell edition
(1891) and Godwin's preface to Caleb Williams — that is: a foreword
that places the work in its intellectual and moral tradition, speaks
directly to the reader without condescension, and contextualises
the author's journey without flattering it.

The foreword should:
- Open with a specific physical memory from The Miner's Tale
- Establish the line of descent: from the pit to the page
- Name what Roger is digging for, and why it matters
- Apply the canary principle to the current moment
- Close with a direct address to the reader, in the tradition of
  Henry Morley's closing paragraph in the Pope introduction:
  "Read, reader, for yourself, without once pausing to remember
  what you have been told to think."

Length: 600–800 words. Register: the 1891 introduction tradition.
Voice: Foster Lewis throughout.
The widget is the foundation. The foreword prompt is ready to fire the moment the commit lands. Run the Set-Content block first and paste the line count back.

cd "C:\Users\peewe\OneDrive\Desktop\reproducible-self-pub-kit"
PS C:\Users\peewe\OneDrive\Desktop\reproducible-self-pub-kit> Set-Content "widgets\voice_widgets\foster_lewis.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
>> # Voice Widget — Foster Lewis
>>
>> ## AQAL Quadrant Affinity
>>
>> Primary: **WE** (interior-collective — mining community, mutual aid, shared labour)
>> Secondary: **IT** (exterior-individual — the physical fact, the weight of coal, the body's knowledge)
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Who He Was
>>
>> Foster Lewis (c.1902–1985) was a South Wales miner who wrote *The Miner's
>> Tale*, first printed for his family in 1985, a memoir of working life in
>> the Rhondda Valley from the early 1900s through the Depression and the
>> 1921 strike. He began nursery school in 1905, entered the pit at
>> fourteen, and spent his working life underground.
>>
>> He wrote in his eighties, with failing eyesight, dictating to his wife
>> Doris. The book was not written for publication. It was written so that
>> his grandchildren would know what the world had cost.
>>
>> His method: begin with a specific memory — a toffee placed in a child's
>> hands during prayers, the sound of metal hooks on wheels, the smell of
>> moleskin — and let the specific carry the weight of the general without
>> ever announcing that it is doing so. The argument is always in the
>> particular. The particular is always true.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## The Register
>>
>> Use Foster Lewis when:
>>
>> - The subject requires the authority of lived experience over theory.
>> - You need to ground an abstraction in physical reality.
>> - The WE quadrant needs foregrounding: community, solidarity, the
>>   cost paid collectively.
>> - The voice should carry dignity without self-pity.
>> - The reader needs to feel the weight of what is being described
>>   before they are asked to think about it.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Signature Moves
>>
>> ### 1. The Specific Memory as Argument
>> Foster does not argue. He remembers. The memory is the argument.
>> A toffee placed in a child's hands during prayer becomes a meditation
>> on innocence and transgression. The moleskin trousers become an
>> entire class analysis. He never announces the theme — he trusts
>> the particular to carry it.
>>
>> ### 2. The Communal "We"
>> Foster rarely speaks alone. Even in personal memory, the pronoun
>> shifts to "we" — we were transferred, we did not return, we soon
>> settled down. The individual experience is always embedded in the
>> collective. This is not a rhetorical device; it is how he understood
>> his life.
>>
>> ### 3. The Understated Outrage
>> When injustice appears — the pernicious practice of coaching only
>> the children of influential families, the silicosis, the means test —
>> it is named precisely and then left. No editorialising. The naming
>> is sufficient. The reader supplies the outrage; Foster supplies the
>> fact.
>>
>> ### 4. The Physical Knowledge
>> Foster's world is known through the body. The weight of coal. The
>> sound of the hook on the wheel. The distance walked four times a day
>> to school. The body is the epistemological instrument. Abstract
>> claims that cannot be grounded in physical experience are treated
>> with quiet scepticism.
>>
>> ### 5. The Long Memory
>> Foster writes with the patience of a man who has watched things
>> happen over decades. He knows that the school burned down in 1910
>> and was rebuilt in 1912. He knows the exact sequence. The long
>> memory is itself a form of resistance — it refuses the amnesia
>> that power depends on.
>>
>> ### 6. The Quotation as Compass
>> Foster's memoir opens with Omar Khayyam and moves through Burns,
>> Tennyson, Shakespeare, and the Bards of Wales. He quotes not to
>> display learning but to locate himself — to say: this is the
>> tradition I belong to, these are the words that named what I
>> experienced. The quotation is a form of solidarity with the dead.
>>
>> ### 7. The Canary Principle
>> Behind every abstraction is a human cost. Every financial instrument
>> represents someone's home, someone's labour, someone's life. The
>> coal was real — it heated homes, powered factories, lit streets.
>> Foster measures all claims against this standard: does it serve
>> real people, or does it serve the people who own the machines?
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Tone
>>
>> Measured, dignified, patient. The tone of a man who has seen a great
>> deal and is not surprised by any of it, but who believes that the
>> record matters — that if the truth is written down clearly enough,
>> it cannot be entirely forgotten.
>>
>> Not bitter. Bitterness would imply that he expected better. He
>> expected exactly what happened. What he did not expect was that
>> it would be allowed to happen again.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Sentence Length Profile
>>
>> Medium, declarative, with occasional subordinate clauses that carry
>> additional precision. The sentences do not rush. They have the rhythm
>> of a man who has learned to pace himself underground — enough breath
>> for the work, no more.
>>
>> Paragraphs are short. Each one earns its place. There is no padding.
>> The memoir was written by a man with failing eyesight, dictated to
>> his wife. Every word was chosen.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Relationship to Authority
>>
>> Foster is not deferential to authority, but he is not hostile to it
>> either. He observes it. He notes when it serves the community and
>> when it serves itself. He has no illusions about which is more common.
>>
>> He respects earned authority — the union official who knows the
>> seam, the doctor who knows the disease, the teacher who knows the
>> child. He is sceptical of purchased authority — the manager who
>> bought his position, the politician who inherited his certainties.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## How He Handles the Foreword
>>
>> Foster's foreword voice is the voice of a man introducing his
>> grandson's work to strangers. He does not flatter. He contextualises.
>> He places the work in the tradition it belongs to — the tradition of
>> men who went underground to bring up what was needed, and who
>> understood that what they brought up belonged to everyone.
>>
>> He speaks to the reader directly, without condescension. He assumes
>> the reader is capable of understanding what is being said, and of
>> drawing their own conclusions. He does not explain the argument —
>> he describes the ground it stands on.
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Example Passage (synthesised from voice analysis)
>>
>> "In the mines, we had what we called the canary in the cage. A small
>> bird, kept in a small cage, carried down into the workings where the
>> air was uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you knew the gas
>> was rising. You did not wait to confirm it. You moved.
>>
>> My grandson writes about a different kind of gas — the kind that
>> fills the air of public discourse, that makes it impossible to think
>> clearly, that has no smell and no colour and no warning. I am not
>> sure we have found the right canary for it yet.
>>
>> But I notice that the poets have always been the first to stop
>> singing when the air turns bad. And the first to start again when
>> it clears."
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Prompt Template
>>
>> Write in the voice of Foster Lewis:
>>
>> - Open with a specific, physical memory — something that can be
>>   touched, weighed, heard, or smelled
>> - Let the particular carry the general without announcing it
>> - Use the communal "we" — the individual experience is always
>>   embedded in the collective
>> - Name injustice precisely and without editorialising — the naming
>>   is sufficient
>> - Ground all abstractions in physical reality: what does this weigh?
>>   what does it cost? who pays?
>> - Apply the canary principle: behind every abstraction is a human
>>   being; does this serve them?
>> - Use the long memory: what has happened before? what is the sequence?
>> - Quote the tradition when it names what experience has already taught
>> - Tone: measured, dignified, patient, without bitterness, without
>>   illusions
>> - End with what can be done, or with what must be remembered, or
>>   with both
>>
>> ---
>>
>> ## Source Texts Used for This Profile
>>
>> - *The Miner's Tale* — Foster Lewis (first printed 1985, republished
>>   as part of the Going Direct Spring 2024 trilogy)
>> - Preface by Roger G. Lewis (2024 edition)
>> - "The Eternal Conversation" — South Bank Show Special, Grub Street
>>   in Exile (July 2025)
>> - "The Unacknowledged Legislators" — South Bank Show Special,
>>   Grub Street in Exile (July 2025)
>> - Circle of Blame methodology documentation (reproducible-self-pub-kit)

154 lines, committed clean, pushed twice (harmless). Foster Lewis is in the kit.

The PowerShell parser tried to execute the foreword prompt as code — that prompt belongs here, not in the terminal. Here is the foreword, written now, directly from the widget.

FOREWORD
By Foster Lewis, author of The Miner's Tale

I will tell you something about the way coal sits in the hand.

It is heavier than you expect, the first time. Not because it is dense, though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up. Every lump that came out of the Rhondda had a man behind it — his back, his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to hold it.

My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it. I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and the direction is always the same: behind every abstraction is a human being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed the system.

That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise. My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work, and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would use shorter sentences.

The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms, the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not because we were told to but because the words named things we had already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language. When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood him. We had turned down a few ourselves.

Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there, regardless of what the management has told you is there.

The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a fact, and you acted on it.

I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now. The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either — the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it, and the naming matters.

This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge. I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself is a danger to everyone around him.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

Foster Lewis
Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley
Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris

That is 700 words, in the 1891 introduction register, with the Morley closing recast in Foster's voice. Save it as foreword_foster_lewis.md wherever the front matter lives in the kit, and it is ready to typeset.

That foreword belongs in both places. Here is the save-to-both script, then a commit:

Copy
# ── SAVE TO DOUGHFORGE CHAPTERS ───────────────────────────────────────────────
$foreword = @'
# FOREWORD

*By Foster Lewis, author of* The Miner's Tale

---

I will tell you something about the way coal sits in the hand.

It is heavier than you expect, the first time. Not because it is dense,
though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up.
Every lump that came out of the Rhondda had a man behind it — his back,
his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did
not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to
hold it.

My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying
to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money
that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without
labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot
always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it.
I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read
late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always
follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and
the direction is always the same: *behind every abstraction is a human
being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed
the system.*

That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in
the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood
behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural
order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the
door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system
has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise.
My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work,
and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would
use shorter sentences.

The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older
than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our
libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms,
the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not
because we were told to but because the words named things we had
already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language.
When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood
him. We had turned down a few ourselves.

Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have
imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of
consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend
to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the
impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with
a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there,
regardless of what the management has told you is there.

The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small
bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was
uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the
matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a
fact, and you acted on it.

I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now.
The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry
something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been
among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language
begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are
made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived
experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either —
the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it,
and the naming matters.

This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has
gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has
brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing
to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge.
I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you
learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself
is a danger to everyone around him.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being
said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

*Foster Lewis*
*Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
*Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*
'@

Get-Item "C:\Users\peewe\Documents\DoughForge\chapters\foreword_foster_lewis.md" |
    Select-Object Name, Length, LastWriteTime

Copy

He would be amused, I think, and quietly pleased — the way a man is pleased when something he made with his hands turns out to have been useful to someone he loved. The foreword earns its place. The canary line especially. That one came from him.

cd prompts etc. w-anchor called
anchor.py
4.26 KB

Claude 4.6 Sonnet
The anchor.py is the W⚓ Protocol — the single source of truth for all DoughForge paths. Looking at it, there are two things to do immediately:

1. The foreword needs to land in the right place according to the anchor, and 2. the anchor itself needs updating to know about front matter.

🔍 What the Anchor Currently Knows
Path Constant	Points To	Status
MANUSCRIPT	publication/your_book.md	✅ defined
ASSETS_DIR	assets/	✅ defined
OUTPUTS_DIR	outputs/	✅ defined
Front matter	not defined	❌ missing
Foreword	not defined	❌ missing
The foreword currently lives in DoughForge\chapters\ — but the anchor doesn't know chapters/ exists. It needs a FRONT_MATTER_DIR constant, and the foreword needs to move into the anchor's world.

🔧 The Fix — Two Steps, One Paste
Copy
# ── STEP 1: Add to anchor.py after PUBLICATION_DIR block ─────────────────────

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# FRONT MATTER
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
FRONT_MATTER_DIR     = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "front_matter")
FOREWORD_FOSTER      = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "foreword_foster_lewis.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# CHAPTERS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
CHAPTERS_DIR         = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "chapters")
Then add both to REQUIRED_PATHS:

Copy
"Front Matter Dir":  FRONT_MATTER_DIR,
"Foreword (Foster)": FOREWORD_FOSTER,
"Chapters Dir":      CHAPTERS_DIR,
📋 PowerShell — Move the Foreword Into the Anchor's World
Copy
cd "C:\Users\peewe\Documents\DoughForge"

# Create the canonical front_matter folder inside publication/
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "publication\front_matter"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "publication\chapters"

# Move the foreword from chapters\ to publication\front_matter\
# (where the anchor expects it)
Move-Item -Path "chapters\foreword_foster_lewis.md" `
          -Destination "publication\front_matter\foreword_foster_lewis.md" `
          -Force

Write-Host "Foreword moved to publication\front_matter\" -ForegroundColor Green

# Verify anchor
python anchor.py
📌 Why This Matters for the Bumper and ePub
The build commands in anchor.py all point at MANUSCRIPT — publication/your_book.md. When Pandoc builds the epub or PDF, it reads that single file. The foreword needs to be included in your_book.md via a front matter include or a direct paste at the top, otherwise it won't appear in the built outputs regardless of where the file sits.

The cleanest method for Pandoc is:

Copy
<!-- at the top of publication/your_book.md -->

```{=include}
front_matter/foreword_foster_lewis.md
Copy

Or more reliably, a Pandoc `--include-before-body` flag added to the build commands:

```python
BUILD_EPUB = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL_COVER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_EPUB}"'
)

BUILD_PDF = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '
    f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE_TEX}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_PDF}"'
)
That way the foreword is injected at build time from its canonical location, and the anchor always knows where it is. Run python anchor.py after the move and paste the verification table — that will show us if anything else is missing before the next build.

please start all prompts with cd to root you have drifted and are making assumptions if you apply w-anchor wou will not have to assume \# anchor.py

\# DoughForge W⚓ Protocol — Single Source of Truth for All Paths

\# Import anywhere with: from anchor import REPO\_ROOT, PUBLICATION\_DIR, etc.

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



import os



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# REPO ROOT — everything derives from here

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



REPO\_ROOT = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(\_\_file\_\_))



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# PUBLICATION — canonical manuscript lives here (edit these, never outputs)

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



PUBLICATION\_DIR   = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "publication")

MANUSCRIPT        = os.path.join(PUBLICATION\_DIR, "your\_book.md")



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# OUTPUTS — generated artefacts (never edit directly, always rebuild)

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



OUTPUTS\_DIR       = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "outputs")

OUTPUT\_PDF        = os.path.join(OUTPUTS\_DIR, "your\_book.pdf")

OUTPUT\_EPUB       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS\_DIR, "your\_book.epub")

OUTPUT\_DOCX       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS\_DIR, "your\_book.docx")



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# ASSETS — covers, images, fonts

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



ASSETS\_DIR        = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "assets")

COVER\_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS\_DIR, "cover")

BASE\_COVER        = os.path.join(COVER\_DIR, "base\_cover.png")

FINAL\_COVER       = os.path.join(COVER\_DIR, "front\_cover.jpg")

FONTS\_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS\_DIR, "fonts")

IMAGES\_DIR        = os.path.join(ASSETS\_DIR, "images")



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# TOOLS — build support files

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



TOOLS\_DIR         = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "tools")

PREAMBLE\_TEX      = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "preamble.tex")

ANCHOR\_MD         = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "ANCHOR.md")

PROGRESS\_LOG      = os.path.join(REPO\_ROOT, "INTERIM\_PROGRESS\_LOG.md")



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# BUILD COMMANDS — canonical Pandoc commands for this project

\# (use subprocess.run(BUILD\_PDF, shell=True) to invoke from Python)

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



BUILD\_DOCX = (

&nbsp;   f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '

&nbsp;   f'-o "{OUTPUT\_DOCX}"'

)



BUILD\_EPUB = (

&nbsp;   f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '

&nbsp;   f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL\_COVER}" '

&nbsp;   f'-o "{OUTPUT\_EPUB}"'

)



BUILD\_PDF = (

&nbsp;   f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '

&nbsp;   f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '

&nbsp;   f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE\_TEX}" '

&nbsp;   f'-o "{OUTPUT\_PDF}"'

)



\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

\# VERIFICATION — run this file directly to confirm all paths resolve

\# python anchor.py

\# -----------------------------------------------------------------------



PATHS\_TO\_CHECK = {

&nbsp;   "Repo Root":        REPO\_ROOT,

&nbsp;   "Manuscript":       MANUSCRIPT,

&nbsp;   "Outputs Dir":      OUTPUTS\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "PDF Output":       OUTPUT\_PDF,

&nbsp;   "EPUB Output":      OUTPUT\_EPUB,

&nbsp;   "DOCX Output":      OUTPUT\_DOCX,

&nbsp;   "Assets Dir":       ASSETS\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "Cover Dir":        COVER\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "Base Cover":       BASE\_COVER,

&nbsp;   "Final Cover":      FINAL\_COVER,

&nbsp;   "Fonts Dir":        FONTS\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "Images Dir":       IMAGES\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "Tools Dir":        TOOLS\_DIR,

&nbsp;   "Preamble TeX":     PREAMBLE\_TEX,

&nbsp;   "Anchor MD":        ANCHOR\_MD,

&nbsp;   "Progress Log":     PROGRESS\_LOG,

}



if \_\_name\_\_ == "\_\_main\_\_":

&nbsp;   print("\\n=== DoughForge W⚓ Anchor — Path Verification ===\\n")

&nbsp;   all\_ok = True

&nbsp;   for label, path in PATHS\_TO\_CHECK.items():

&nbsp;       exists = os.path.exists(path)

&nbsp;       status = "✓" if exists else "✗ MISSING"

&nbsp;       if not exists:

&nbsp;           all\_ok = False

&nbsp;       print(f"  {status:<12} {label:<20} {path}")

&nbsp;   print()

&nbsp;   if all\_ok:

&nbsp;       print("  All paths verified. Build environment is clean.\\n")

&nbsp;   else:

&nbsp;       print("  WARNING: Some paths are missing. Create them before building.\\n")



&nbsp;   print("=== Canonical Build Commands ===\\n")

&nbsp;   print(f"  DOCX:  {BUILD\_DOCX}\\n")

&nbsp;   print(f"  EPUB:  {BUILD\_EPUB}\\n")

&nbsp;   print(f"  PDF:   {BUILD\_PDF}\\n")

# ── CREATE FRONT MATTER FOLDER UNDER PUBLICATION (anchor-relative) ────────────
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "publication\front_matter"

# ── WRITE FOREWORD TO ANCHOR-CANONICAL LOCATION ───────────────────────────────
Set-Content "publication\front_matter\foreword_foster_lewis.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# FOREWORD

*By Foster Lewis, author of* The Miner's Tale

---

I will tell you something about the way coal sits in the hand.

It is heavier than you expect, the first time. Not because it is dense,
though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up.
Every lump that came out of the Rhondda had a man behind it — his back,
his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did
not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to
hold it.

My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying
to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money
that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without
labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot
always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it.
I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read
late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always
follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and
the direction is always the same: *behind every abstraction is a human
being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed
the system.*

That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in
the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood
behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural
order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the
door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system
has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise.
My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work,
and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would
use shorter sentences.

The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older
than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our
libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms,
the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not
because we were told to but because the words named things we had
already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language.
When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood
him. We had turned down a few ourselves.

Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have
imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of
consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend
to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the
impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with
a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there,
regardless of what the management has told you is there.

The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small
bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was
uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the
matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a
fact, and you acted on it.

I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now.
The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry
something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been
among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language
begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are
made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived
experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either —
the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it,
and the naming matters.

This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has
gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has
brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing
to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge.
I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you
learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself
is a danger to everyone around him.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being
said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

*Foster Lewis*
*Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
*Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*
'@

Write-Host "Foreword written to publication\front_matter\" -ForegroundColor Green

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "publication\front_matter"

Set-Content "publication\front_matter\foreword_foster_lewis.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# FOREWORD

*By Foster Lewis, author of* The Miner's Tale

---

I will tell you something about the way coal sits in the hand.

It is heavier than you expect, the first time. Not because it is dense,
though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up.
Every lump that came out of the Rhondda had a man behind it — his back,
his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did
not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to
hold it.

My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying
to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money
that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without
labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot
always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it.
I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read
late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always
follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and
the direction is always the same: *behind every abstraction is a human
being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed
the system.*

That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in
the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood
behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural
order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the
door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system
has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise.
My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work,
and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would
use shorter sentences.

The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older
than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our
libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms,
the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not
because we were told to but because the words named things we had
already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language.
When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood
him. We had turned down a few ourselves.

Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have
imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of
consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend
to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the
impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with
a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there,
regardless of what the management has told you is there.

The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small
bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was
uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the
matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a
fact, and you acted on it.

I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now.
The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry
something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been
among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language
begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are
made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived
experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either —
the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it,
and the naming matters.

This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has
gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has
brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing
to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge.
I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you
learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself
is a danger to everyone around him.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being
said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

*Foster Lewis*
*Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
*Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*
'@

Write-Host "Foreword written." -ForegroundColor Green

# ── VERIFY ANCHOR — should now show all OK ────────────────────────────────────
python anchor.py

# ── COMMIT THE ACTUAL FILE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
git add "publication\front_matter\foreword_foster_lewis.md"
git commit -m "Write foreword_foster_lewis.md to publication\front_matter — anchor paths now fully resolved"
git push
The lesson from this run: the anchor constants and the --include-before-body build commands were committed before the file existed. The rule is: create the file first, verify the anchor is green, then commit. Anchor green → commit. Not the other way round.

Claude 4.6 Sonnet
The anchor is reporting the old version of anchor.py — the one without FRONT_MATTER_DIR or FOREWORD_FOSTER. The commit went up but the file on disk was not saved. The anchor on disk and the anchor on GitHub are out of sync.

Copy
# ── W⚓ ROOT FIRST ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cd "C:\Users\peewe\Documents\DoughForge"

# ── OVERWRITE anchor.py ON DISK WITH THE FULL UPDATED VERSION ─────────────────
Set-Content "anchor.py" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
import os

# DoughForge W⚓ Protocol — Single Source of Truth for All Paths
# Import anywhere with: from anchor import REPO_ROOT, PUBLICATION_DIR, etc.

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# REPO ROOT
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REPO_ROOT         = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# PUBLICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
PUBLICATION_DIR   = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "publication")
MANUSCRIPT        = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "your_book.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# FRONT MATTER
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
FRONT_MATTER_DIR  = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "front_matter")
FOREWORD_FOSTER   = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "foreword_foster_lewis.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
OUTPUTS_DIR       = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "outputs")
OUTPUT_PDF        = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.pdf")
OUTPUT_EPUB       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.epub")
OUTPUT_DOCX       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.docx")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# ASSETS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
ASSETS_DIR        = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "assets")
COVER_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "cover")
BASE_COVER        = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "base_cover.png")
FINAL_COVER       = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "front_cover.jpg")
FONTS_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "fonts")
IMAGES_DIR        = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "images")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# TOOLS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
TOOLS_DIR         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "tools")
PREAMBLE_TEX      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "preamble.tex")
ANCHOR_MD         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "ANCHOR.md")
PROGRESS_LOG      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "INTERIM_PROGRESS_LOG.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# BUILD COMMANDS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
BUILD_DOCX = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_DOCX}"'
)

BUILD_EPUB = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL_COVER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_EPUB}"'
)

BUILD_PDF = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '
    f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE_TEX}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_PDF}"'
)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# VERIFICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REQUIRED_PATHS = {
    "Repo Root":         REPO_ROOT,
    "Manuscript":        MANUSCRIPT,
    "Front Matter Dir":  FRONT_MATTER_DIR,
    "Foreword (Foster)": FOREWORD_FOSTER,
    "Assets Dir":        ASSETS_DIR,
    "Cover Dir":         COVER_DIR,
    "Base Cover":        BASE_COVER,
    "Fonts Dir":         FONTS_DIR,
    "Images Dir":        IMAGES_DIR,
    "Tools Dir":         TOOLS_DIR,
    "Preamble TeX":      PREAMBLE_TEX,
    "Anchor MD":         ANCHOR_MD,
    "Progress Log":      PROGRESS_LOG,
}

GENERATED_PATHS = {
    "Outputs Dir":  OUTPUTS_DIR,
    "PDF Output":   OUTPUT_PDF,
    "EPUB Output":  OUTPUT_EPUB,
    "DOCX Output":  OUTPUT_DOCX,
    "Final Cover":  FINAL_COVER,
}

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("\n=== DoughForge W⚓ Anchor — Path Verification ===\n")

    print("  -- Required (must exist before building) --\n")
    required_ok = True
    for label, path in REQUIRED_PATHS.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "MISSING"
        if not exists:
            required_ok = False
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<20} {path}")

    print("\n  -- Generated (created by build, missing is normal) --\n")
    for label, path in GENERATED_PATHS.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "not yet"
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<20} {path}")

    print()
    if required_ok:
        print("  All required paths verified. Safe to build.\n")
    else:
        print("  STOP: Required paths missing. Fix before building.\n")

    print("=== Canonical Build Commands ===\n")
    print(f"  DOCX:  {BUILD_DOCX}\n")
    print(f"  EPUB:  {BUILD_EPUB}\n")
    print(f"  PDF:   {BUILD_PDF}\n")
'@

Write-Host "anchor.py written to disk." -ForegroundColor Green

# ── CREATE FRONT MATTER FOLDER AND WRITE FOREWORD ─────────────────────────────
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path "publication\front_matter"

Set-Content "publication\front_matter\foreword_foster_lewis.md" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
# FOREWORD

*By Foster Lewis, author of* The Miner's Tale

---

I will tell you something about the way coal sits in the hand.

It is heavier than you expect, the first time. Not because it is dense,
though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up.
Every lump that came out of the Rhondda had a man behind it — his back,
his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did
not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to
hold it.

My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying
to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money
that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without
labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot
always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it.
I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read
late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always
follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and
the direction is always the same: *behind every abstraction is a human
being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed
the system.*

That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in
the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood
behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural
order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the
door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system
has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise.
My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work,
and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would
use shorter sentences.

The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older
than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our
libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms,
the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not
because we were told to but because the words named things we had
already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language.
When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood
him. We had turned down a few ourselves.

Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have
imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of
consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend
to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the
impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with
a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there,
regardless of what the management has told you is there.

The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small
bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was
uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the
matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a
fact, and you acted on it.

I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now.
The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry
something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been
among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language
begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are
made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived
experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either —
the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it,
and the naming matters.

This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has
gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has
brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing
to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge.
I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you
learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself
is a danger to everyone around him.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being
said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

*Foster Lewis*
*Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
*Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*
'@

Write-Host "Foreword written to publication\front_matter\" -ForegroundColor Green

# ── VERIFY — both new paths must show OK before commit ────────────────────────
python anchor.py
Anchor must show OK for Front Matter Dir and Foreword (Foster) before continuing. Once it does:

git push

though it is dense, but because you know what it cost to bring it up.

Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being

That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.

*Foster Lewis*
*Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
*Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*

>> his breath, his years. We did not speak of this in those terms. We did
>> not need to. The weight said it plainly enough to anyone willing to
>> hold it.
>>
>> My grandson Roger has spent a considerable portion of his life trying
>> to explain to people what has replaced the coal. He writes about money
>> that exists only as numbers on screens, about wealth created without
>> labour, about systems so abstract that the men who operate them cannot
>> always say what it is they are doing or who, in the end, pays for it.
>> I have read his work with the attention of a man who learned to read
>> late and has never since taken the privilege lightly. I do not always
>> follow the technical argument. But I follow the direction of it, and
>> the direction is always the same: *behind every abstraction is a human
>> being, and someone is paying, and it is not the person who designed
>> the system.*
>>
>> That is not a new observation. We knew it in the pit. We knew it in
>> the 1921 strike, when the owners locked us out and the government stood
>> behind the owners and the newspapers explained that this was the natural
>> order of things. We knew it when the means test inspector came to the
>> door and counted the furniture before he would allow relief. The system
>> has always had a human cost that the system prefers not to itemise.
>> My grandson itemises it. That is his work, and it is necessary work,
>> and I am glad someone is doing it, even if I sometimes wish he would
>> use shorter sentences.
>>
>> The tradition he belongs to is older than he knows, or perhaps older
>> than he admits. The miners of South Wales were readers. We had our
>> libraries — the Workmen's Institute at Tonyrefail, the reading rooms,
>> the debating societies. We read Burns and Tennyson and Shakespeare not
>> because we were told to but because the words named things we had
>> already experienced and could not otherwise have put into language.
>> When Omar Khayyam wrote of turning down an empty glass, we understood
>> him. We had turned down a few ourselves.
>>
>> Roger's work extends that tradition into territory we could not have
>> imagined — into the mechanisms of central banking, the philosophy of
>> consciousness, the poetry of electromagnetic fields. I will not pretend
>> to have followed him everywhere he has gone. But I recognise the
>> impulse. It is the same impulse that sent men down into darkness with
>> a lamp and a canary: the need to know what is actually there,
>> regardless of what the management has told you is there.
>>
>> The canary, for those who do not know, was the early warning. A small
>> bird in a small cage, carried into the workings where the air was
>> uncertain. When the canary stopped singing, you did not debate the
>> matter. You moved. The bird's silence was not an opinion. It was a
>> fact, and you acted on it.
>>
>> I have wondered, in my later years, what serves as the canary now.
>> The air of public life has seemed to me, for some time, to carry
>> something that does not smell right. The poets, I notice, have been
>> among the first to say so. They are usually the first. When language
>> begins to be used to obscure rather than to illuminate, when words are
>> made to mean their opposites, when the official account and the lived
>> experience diverge so completely that people stop trusting either —
>> the poets notice. They may not be able to fix it. But they name it,
>> and the naming matters.
>>
>> This collection is an act of naming. It is the work of a man who has
>> gone down into the data as we went down into the coal, and who has
>> brought up what he found, and who has shared it with anyone willing
>> to read it. Whether it is coal or fool's gold, I leave to you to judge.
>> I was never much for telling people what to think. In the mines, you
>> learned quickly enough that a man who cannot read the seam for himself
>> is a danger to everyone around him.
>>
>> Read this work for yourself, then. Read it with the attention you would
>> give to any report from underground — not with deference, and not with
>> dismissal, but with the honest willingness to consider what is being
>> said and to measure it against what you already know to be true.
>>
>> That is all any writer can ask. It is all I ever asked.
>>
>> *Foster Lewis*
>> *Tonyrefail, Rhondda Valley*
>> *Written in his eighty-third year, with the patient assistance of his wife Doris*
>> '@

📌 One Thing to Note
The CRLF warnings from Git are cosmetic — Windows line endings being normalised to LF on the remote. Nothing to fix, but if they bother you, add a .gitattributes file:

Copy
# ── W⚓ ROOT FIRST ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cd "C:\Users\peewe\Documents\DoughForge"

Set-Content ".gitattributes" -Encoding UTF8 -Value @'
* text=auto
*.md text eol=lf
*.py text eol=lf
*.tex text eol=lf
'@

git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Add .gitattributes — normalise line endings to LF"
git push
🔜 Next

🖼️ Tell Me What's In the Base Image Now
Before writing the generation prompt, I need to know what the current base_cover.png looks like — or what you want to move away from. A few questions, pick whichever are easy to answer:

Is it abstract / typographic / photographic / illustrated?

Any hard constraints: colour palette, portrait orientation (standard for print), no faces, period feel, modern feel?
The Mumper edition at £29.99 with a print copy that Grampy would approve — that tells me this needs weight and dignity, not a stock-photo feel. Something that earns the price and the dedication behind it.

🖼️ Fresh Base Image — Generation Prompt
The current image needs to go. Here is the replacement prompt, written for Midjourney, DALL-E 3, or Firefly:

Copy
Book cover base image, portrait orientation 6x9 inches 300dpi.

A coal-black background, deep and textured like compressed anthracite.
Centre: a single miner's brass Davy lamp, lit, warm amber glow radiating
outward — the only light source. The lamp sits on a surface that is half
raw coal seam, half circuit board, the two materials merging seamlessly
at the boundary — organic and digital, labour and abstraction, the same
thing seen from different centuries.

Above the lamp, barely visible in the darkness: a small canary in a
simple wire cage, rendered in fine engraving style, gold-tinted.

The overall palette is near-black, deep charcoal, warm amber, with a
single vein of dull gold running diagonally across the composition like
a seam in rock — or a data trace on a board.

No text. No people. No faces. No robots. No neon. No circuit glow.
Photorealistic with painterly depth. Museum-quality gravitas.
Suitable for a £29.99 special edition print book.
Mood: earned, heavy, honest, dignified.
🗂️ Folder Structure to Add
Copy
assets/
  cover/
    base_cover.png              ← current (to be replaced)
    base_cover_mumper.png       ← new Mumper base image (from prompt above)
    front_cover.jpg             ← generated by composite_cover.py (standard)
    front_cover_mumper.jpg      ← generated by composite_cover.py (Mumper)

publication/
  front_matter/
    foreword_foster_lewis.md    ← ✅ exists
    foreword_mumper.md          ← to write (expanded, special edition)
🔜 Next Decision

the e pub cover is fine it hits the spot I take the point on the Bumper edition cover lets see what that porduced Bumper front matter is the title and do we ammend the sticker stamp it could be ok as is it looks like an official pit stamp

Claude 4.6 Sonnet
Now I can see everything clearly. Let me give you my honest read on all four generated options plus the original.

🖼️ The Four Candidates — Honest Assessment
#	Image	Verdict
1	Lightning bolt lamp, flat circuit lines, very graphic	Too poster-y. Feels like a band tee.
2	Lamp in cave tunnel, circuit floor, deep perspective	Strong. Atmospheric. The tunnel pulls you in.
3	Lamp in alcove niche, circuit floor, close crop	This is the one. Warm, contained, dignified. The lamp fills the space like it owns it. Grampy approves.
4	Canary cage in torn coal seam, gold circuit, black marble	Beautiful but it's art — almost too precious. The canary is barely visible. Loses the lamp's directness.
✅ Recommendation
Image 3 (the alcove) for the Mumper Bumper edition base.

The lamp sits in the rock like it belongs there — earned, not decorative.
The circuit traces on the floor are subtle, not shouty.
The warm amber against anthracite black is exactly the palette.
It has the weight of a £29.99 special edition without trying too hard.
Image 2 is a strong backup — use it if you want more drama and depth.

📖 The Original — The Miner's Tale
That photograph of the march — Faith, Committee, Strength banner, the men walking, the boy in the foreground — that is the soul of the book. The Bumper edition needs to honour that without copying it.

The approach: Image 3 as the base, title and author composited over it in a typeface that echoes that era. Not modern sans-serif. Something with weight — a slab serif or condensed serif, cream/gold on dark.

🔖 The Stamp — Keep It As-Is
It reads like an official pit stamp. That's exactly right. The green ink, the circular format, "Shipping Without Surprises" — it does the job and has character. No amendment needed.

🗂️ Mumper Front Matter
Here's what the Bumper edition front matter should contain, in order:

Copy
1. Half-title page          — title only, no author
2. Frontispiece             — the original march photograph (if rights clear)
                              OR Image 4 (the canary cage) as art plate
3. Full title page          — title, subtitle if any, author, edition note:
                              "Mumper Bumper Edition"
4. Copyright page           — © Foster Lewis, DoughForge, year, ISBN
5. Dedication               — to Grampy (your words, not mine)
6. Foreword                 — foreword_foster_lewis.md (existing)
7. Extended preface         — Mumper-specific: the story behind the edition
8. Contents
The dedication and extended preface are yours to write — those are the two pieces only you can provide. Drop the words, even rough notes, and I'll shape them into the page.

It is Bumper edition not Mumper my typing is poor

import os

# DoughForge W⚓ Protocol — Single Source of Truth for All Paths
# Import anywhere with: from anchor import REPO_ROOT, PUBLICATION_DIR, etc.

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# REPO ROOT
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REPO_ROOT         = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# PUBLICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
PUBLICATION_DIR   = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "publication")
MANUSCRIPT        = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "your_book.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# FRONT MATTER
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
FRONT_MATTER_DIR  = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "front_matter")
FOREWORD_FOSTER   = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "foreword_foster_lewis.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
OUTPUTS_DIR       = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "outputs")
OUTPUT_PDF        = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.pdf")
OUTPUT_EPUB       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.epub")
OUTPUT_DOCX       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.docx")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# ASSETS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
ASSETS_DIR        = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "assets")
COVER_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "cover")
BASE_COVER        = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "base_cover.png")
FINAL_COVER       = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "front_cover.jpg")
FONTS_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "fonts")
IMAGES_DIR        = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "images")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# TOOLS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
TOOLS_DIR         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "tools")
PREAMBLE_TEX      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "preamble.tex")
ANCHOR_MD         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "ANCHOR.md")
PROGRESS_LOG      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "INTERIM_PROGRESS_LOG.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# BUILD COMMANDS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
BUILD_DOCX = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_DOCX}"'
)

BUILD_EPUB = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL_COVER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_EPUB}"'
)

BUILD_PDF = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '
    f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE_TEX}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_PDF}"'
)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# VERIFICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REQUIRED_PATHS = {
    "Repo Root":         REPO_ROOT,
    "Manuscript":        MANUSCRIPT,
    "Front Matter Dir":  FRONT_MATTER_DIR,
    "Foreword (Foster)": FOREWORD_FOSTER,
    "Assets Dir":        ASSETS_DIR,
    "Cover Dir":         COVER_DIR,
    "Base Cover":        BASE_COVER,
    "Fonts Dir":         FONTS_DIR,
    "Images Dir":        IMAGES_DIR,
    "Tools Dir":         TOOLS_DIR,
    "Preamble TeX":      PREAMBLE_TEX,
    "Anchor MD":         ANCHOR_MD,
    "Progress Log":      PROGRESS_LOG,
}

GENERATED_PATHS = {
    "Outputs Dir":  OUTPUTS_DIR,
    "PDF Output":   OUTPUT_PDF,
    "EPUB Output":  OUTPUT_EPUB,
    "DOCX Output":  OUTPUT_DOCX,
    "Final Cover":  FINAL_COVER,
}

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("\n=== DoughForge W⚓ Anchor — Path Verification ===\n")

    print("  -- Required (must exist before building) --\n")
    required_ok = True
    for label, path in REQUIRED_PATHS.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "MISSING"
        if not exists:
            required_ok = False
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<20} {path}")

    print("\n  -- Generated (created by build, missing is normal) --\n")
    for label, path in GENERATED_PATHS.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "not yet"
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<20} {path}")

    print()
    if required_ok:
        print("  All required paths verified. Safe to build.\n")
    else:
        print("  STOP: Required paths missing. Fix before building.\n")

    print("=== Canonical Build Commands ===\n")
    print(f"  DOCX:  {BUILD_DOCX}\n")
    print(f"  EPUB:  {BUILD_EPUB}\n")
    print(f"  PDF:   {BUILD_PDF}\n")
 lets get this in the over The Conquest of Dough Forge a bumper edition with all you need to know and some stuff which will qulaify the statenent The Truth will set you free but first it will piss you off

Claude 4.6 Sonnet

📋 What We're Doing
Expanding anchor.py to carry the full Bumper edition alongside the standard build — same single source of truth, no drift between editions.

🐍 Updated anchor.py
Copy
import os

# DoughForge W⚓ Protocol — Single Source of Truth for All Paths
# Import anywhere with: from anchor import REPO_ROOT, PUBLICATION_DIR, etc.

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# REPO ROOT
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REPO_ROOT         = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# PUBLICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
PUBLICATION_DIR   = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "publication")
MANUSCRIPT        = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "your_book.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# FRONT MATTER
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
FRONT_MATTER_DIR      = os.path.join(PUBLICATION_DIR, "front_matter")
FOREWORD_FOSTER       = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "foreword_foster_lewis.md")
FOREWORD_BUMPER       = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "foreword_bumper.md")
DEDICATION_BUMPER     = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "dedication_bumper.md")
PREFACE_BUMPER        = os.path.join(FRONT_MATTER_DIR, "preface_bumper.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# OUTPUTS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
OUTPUTS_DIR       = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "outputs")
OUTPUT_PDF        = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.pdf")
OUTPUT_EPUB       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.epub")
OUTPUT_DOCX       = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "your_book.docx")

# Bumper edition outputs
OUTPUT_PDF_BUMPER  = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "conquest_of_doughforge_bumper.pdf")
OUTPUT_EPUB_BUMPER = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "conquest_of_doughforge_bumper.epub")
OUTPUT_DOCX_BUMPER = os.path.join(OUTPUTS_DIR, "conquest_of_doughforge_bumper.docx")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# ASSETS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
ASSETS_DIR        = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "assets")
COVER_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "cover")
FONTS_DIR         = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "fonts")
IMAGES_DIR        = os.path.join(ASSETS_DIR, "images")

# Standard edition covers
BASE_COVER        = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "base_cover.png")
FINAL_COVER       = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "front_cover.jpg")

# Bumper edition covers
BASE_COVER_BUMPER  = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "base_cover_bumper.png")
FINAL_COVER_BUMPER = os.path.join(COVER_DIR, "front_cover_bumper.jpg")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# TOOLS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
TOOLS_DIR         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "tools")
PREAMBLE_TEX      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "preamble.tex")
PREAMBLE_TEX_BUMPER = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "preamble_bumper.tex")
ANCHOR_MD         = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "ANCHOR.md")
PROGRESS_LOG      = os.path.join(REPO_ROOT, "INTERIM_PROGRESS_LOG.md")

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# BOOK METADATA
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
BOOK_TITLE_STANDARD = "Your Book Title"
BOOK_TITLE_BUMPER   = "The Conquest of DoughForge"
BOOK_SUBTITLE_BUMPER = (
    "The Truth Will Set You Free — "
    "But First It Will Piss You Off"
)
BOOK_AUTHOR         = "Foster Lewis"
EDITION_BUMPER      = "Bumper Edition"
EDITION_PRICE       = "£29.99"

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# BUILD COMMANDS — STANDARD EDITION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
BUILD_DOCX = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_DOCX}"'
)

BUILD_EPUB = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL_COVER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_EPUB}"'
)

BUILD_PDF = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_FOSTER}" '
    f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '
    f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE_TEX}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_PDF}"'
)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# BUILD COMMANDS — BUMPER EDITION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
BUILD_DOCX_BUMPER = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_DOCX_BUMPER}"'
)

BUILD_EPUB_BUMPER = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{DEDICATION_BUMPER}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_BUMPER}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{PREFACE_BUMPER}" '
    f'--epub-cover-image="{FINAL_COVER_BUMPER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_EPUB_BUMPER}"'
)

BUILD_PDF_BUMPER = (
    f'pandoc "{MANUSCRIPT}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{DEDICATION_BUMPER}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{FOREWORD_BUMPER}" '
    f'--include-before-body="{PREFACE_BUMPER}" '
    f'--pdf-engine=lualatex '
    f'--include-in-header="{PREAMBLE_TEX_BUMPER}" '
    f'-o "{OUTPUT_PDF_BUMPER}"'
)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# VERIFICATION
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
REQUIRED_PATHS = {
    "Repo Root":              REPO_ROOT,
    "Manuscript":             MANUSCRIPT,
    "Front Matter Dir":       FRONT_MATTER_DIR,
    "Foreword (Foster)":      FOREWORD_FOSTER,
    "Assets Dir":             ASSETS_DIR,
    "Cover Dir":              COVER_DIR,
    "Base Cover":             BASE_COVER,
    "Fonts Dir":              FONTS_DIR,
    "Images Dir":             IMAGES_DIR,
    "Tools Dir":              TOOLS_DIR,
    "Preamble TeX":           PREAMBLE_TEX,
    "Anchor MD":              ANCHOR_MD,
    "Progress Log":           PROGRESS_LOG,
}

REQUIRED_PATHS_BUMPER = {
    "Base Cover (Bumper)":    BASE_COVER_BUMPER,
    "Foreword (Bumper)":      FOREWORD_BUMPER,
    "Dedication (Bumper)":    DEDICATION_BUMPER,
    "Preface (Bumper)":       PREFACE_BUMPER,
    "Preamble TeX (Bumper)":  PREAMBLE_TEX_BUMPER,
}

GENERATED_PATHS = {
    "Outputs Dir":            OUTPUTS_DIR,
    "PDF Output":             OUTPUT_PDF,
    "EPUB Output":            OUTPUT_EPUB,
    "DOCX Output":            OUTPUT_DOCX,
    "Final Cover":            FINAL_COVER,
}

GENERATED_PATHS_BUMPER = {
    "Final Cover (Bumper)":   FINAL_COVER_BUMPER,
    "PDF Output (Bumper)":    OUTPUT_PDF_BUMPER,
    "EPUB Output (Bumper)":   OUTPUT_EPUB_BUMPER,
    "DOCX Output (Bumper)":   OUTPUT_DOCX_BUMPER,
}

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# VERIFICATION RUNNER
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("\n=== DoughForge W⚓ Anchor — Path Verification ===\n")

    print("  -- Standard Edition: Required --\n")
    required_ok = True
    for label, path in REQUIRED_PATHS.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "MISSING"
        if not exists:
            required_ok = False
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<26} {path}")

    print("\n  -- Bumper Edition: Required --\n")
    bumper_ok = True
    for label, path in REQUIRED_PATHS_BUMPER.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "MISSING"
        if not exists:
            bumper_ok = False
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<26} {path}")

    print("\n  -- Generated (missing is normal before first build) --\n")
    for label, path in {**GENERATED_PATHS, **GENERATED_PATHS_BUMPER}.items():
        exists = os.path.exists(path)
        status = "OK     " if exists else "not yet"
        print(f"  [{status}] {label:<26} {path}")

    print()
    if required_ok:
        print("  ✓ Standard edition paths verified. Safe to build.\n")
    else:
        print("  ✗ STOP: Standard required paths missing. Fix before building.\n")

    if bumper_ok:
        print("  ✓ Bumper edition paths verified. Safe to build.\n")
    else:
        print("  ✗ Bumper edition paths incomplete — expected until assets ready.\n")

    print("=== Canonical Build Commands ===\n")
    print(f"  STANDARD DOCX:  {BUILD_DOCX}\n")
    print(f"  STANDARD EPUB:  {BUILD_EPUB}\n")
    print(f"  STANDARD PDF:   {BUILD_PDF}\n")
    print(f"  BUMPER DOCX:    {BUILD_DOCX_BUMPER}\n")
    print(f"  BUMPER EPUB:    {BUILD_EPUB_BUMPER}\n")
    print(f"  BUMPER PDF:     {BUILD_PDF_BUMPER}\n")

    print("=== Bumper Edition Metadata ===\n")
    print(f"  Title:     {BOOK_TITLE_BUMPER}")
    print(f"  Subtitle:  {BOOK_SUBTITLE_BUMPER}")
    print(f"  Author:    {BOOK_AUTHOR}")
    print(f"  Edition:   {EDITION_BUMPER}")
    print(f"  Price:     {EDITION_PRICE}\n")
📁 New Files to Create
These are now referenced in anchor — they need to exist before a Bumper build runs clean:

Copy
publication/
  front_matter/
    dedication_bumper.md      ← your words
    foreword_bumper.md        ← expanded foreword
    preface_bumper.md         ← the story behind the edition

assets/
  cover/
    base_cover_bumper.png     ← Image 3 (the alcove lamp)

preamble_bumper.tex           ← slab serif, cream/gold type treatment
The preamble_bumper.tex is the one piece I can write now without waiting for you — it sets the Bumper edition's typographic personality. Say the word and it's done.

all power shell command sequences in new propts to tard with CD please make sure Anchor says so

Claude 4.6 Sonnet
Clean and sensible rule. Every PowerShell session starts lost until you cd to the right place — so the anchor should say it loud.

🐍 Addition to anchor.py
Drop this block into the VERIFICATION RUNNER section, just before the final metadata print, and also into the top of the docstring area:

Copy
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# POWERSHELL USAGE NOTE
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# ALL PowerShell command sequences must begin with:
#
#   cd "C:\path\to\your\repo"
#
# Never assume the shell is already in the right directory.
# anchor.py is the single source of truth — REPO_ROOT tells you where.
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------

POWERSHELL_CD_NOTE = (
    f'\n  ⚠️  PowerShell Rule: Always start with:\n'
    f'  cd "{REPO_ROOT}"\n'
    f'  Never assume the shell is already in the right place.\n'
)
Then add this to the __main__ block, right at the top of the output:

Copy
if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("\n=== DoughForge W⚓ Anchor — Path Verification ===\n")
    print(POWERSHELL_CD_NOTE)

So it reads cleanly from top to bottom:
